P3chat.history: P3chat is a small live chat SaaS platform for website customer support with no notable ownership changes or significant milestones publicly documented.
P3chat.summary_abuse: P3chat is a small live chat tool with no notable abuse signal.

p5.js.history: p5.js was created by Lauren McCarthy in 2014 as a JavaScript reimplementation of Processing, the creative coding environment originally developed by Ben Fry and Casey Reas in Java. It is maintained by the Processing Foundation and widely used in creative coding and education.
p5.js.summary_abuse: p5.js has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side creative coding library used in art and education.

Pabbly Form Builder.history: Pabbly is an Indian SaaS company founded around 2016 providing a suite of business tools including form builders, email marketing, subscription management, and workflow automation. Pabbly Form Builder is one product within the broader Pabbly platform.
Pabbly Form Builder.summary_abuse: Pabbly Form Builder has no notable abuse signal beyond what is typical for form builder platforms. Forms that collect user data can be embedded on phishing pages to harvest credentials, though no specific incidents involving Pabbly have been documented and legitimate use dominates.

Pace.history: Pace (PacePay) is a Buy Now Pay Later platform focused on Southeast Asian markets, founded around 2018. It provides BNPL payment solutions for merchants in Singapore and other Asian markets.
Pace.summary_abuse: Pace has no notable abuse signal beyond what is typical for BNPL payment services. BNPL platforms can be targeted for payment fraud and account takeover, but Pace serves legitimate merchants and no specific fraud incidents have been publicly documented.

Package.AI.history: Package.AI is a last-mile delivery and customer engagement platform using AI to optimize delivery communications and service interactions for ecommerce businesses.
Package.AI.summary_abuse: Package.AI has no notable abuse signal. It is a last-mile delivery communication platform for ecommerce merchants with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraud operators would target.

Packery.history: Packery is a JavaScript layout library and jQuery plugin created by David DeSandro (Metafizzy) around 2013, enabling draggable and gapless masonry-style layouts for web pages. It is part of the Metafizzy layout library family that includes Masonry and Isotope.
Packery.summary_abuse: Packery has no notable abuse signal. It is a JavaScript layout library for web developers with no consumer-facing surface that fraud operators would target.

Packlink PRO.history: Packlink was founded in 2012 in Spain as a multi-carrier shipping marketplace for SMBs and ecommerce merchants, allowing comparison and booking of parcel deliveries from multiple carriers. It was acquired by Auctane (parent of Stamps.com and ShipStation) around 2021.
Packlink PRO.summary_abuse: Packlink PRO has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B shipping management platform used by legitimate ecommerce merchants to manage parcel deliveries, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraud operators would target.

Packman.history: Packman.ai is an AI-powered ecommerce enablement platform launched around 2022, targeting online store operators with automation features.
Packman.summary_abuse: Packman.ai is a small AI-ecommerce platform with no documented abuse signal.

Paddle.history: Paddle was founded in 2012 in London as a merchant of record and payment infrastructure provider for software companies. It acquired ProfitWell in 2022, integrating subscription analytics into its platform. Paddle operates as the merchant of record for its customers, handling global tax compliance, payment processing, and subscription management.
Paddle.summary_abuse: Paddle has no notable abuse signal beyond what is typical for any payment infrastructure provider. As a merchant of record, Paddle performs KYC and fraud screening on its software vendors. No specific public abuse campaigns or scam patterns specific to Paddle have been documented.

Paessler.history: Paessler AG was founded in 1997 in Nuremberg, Germany, and developed PRTG Network Monitor, a comprehensive network infrastructure monitoring tool. The company has grown to serve thousands of enterprise customers globally and remains privately held and independent.
Paessler.summary_abuse: Paessler has no notable abuse signal. It is a network monitoring platform used by IT infrastructure teams within enterprise organizations, with no consumer-facing surface that fraud operators would target.

Pagar.me.history: Pagar.me was founded in 2013 in Brazil as a developer-focused payment processing API for the Brazilian market. The company was acquired by Stone Co. (a publicly traded Brazilian payment company listed on Nasdaq) in 2016, expanding its reach within the Brazilian fintech ecosystem.
Pagar.me.summary_abuse: Pagar.me has no notable abuse signal beyond what is typical for payment processors. It is a Brazilian payment API serving legitimate online businesses, with PCI DSS compliance as a baseline control.

Page Builder Framework.history: Page Builder Framework is a lightweight WordPress theme designed to work with page builders like Elementor, Beaver Builder, and Divi. It was developed by a German indie developer around 2017 and is available on WordPress.org with a premium add-on.
Page Builder Framework.summary_abuse: Page Builder Framework has no notable abuse signal. It is a lightweight WordPress theme used as a foundation for page builder sites, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraud operators would target.

Page.js.history: Page.js is a lightweight client-side router library for Node.js/browser, created by TJ Holowaychuk (visionmedia) around 2012. It was popular in the early single-page application era but has largely been superseded by React Router and other modern routing libraries.
Page.js.summary_abuse: Page.js has no notable abuse signal. It is a JavaScript client-side routing library with no consumer-facing surface that fraud operators would target.

Pagecloud.history: Pagecloud was founded in 2014 as a Canadian drag-and-drop website builder targeting small businesses and designers. It has remained independent and active with a freemium model and no notable ownership changes.
Pagecloud.summary_abuse: Pagecloud is a website builder SaaS with no notable abuse signal. Like other website builders, it could theoretically be used to host scam pages, but there are no documented patterns of significant misuse associated with this platform specifically.

Pagefai CMS.history: Pagefai CMS is a cloud-based SaaS content management and ecommerce platform with no notable public ownership history documented.
Pagefai CMS.summary_abuse: Pagefai CMS is a niche cloud CMS with no notable abuse signal.

PageFly.history: PageFly was founded in 2017 and launched on the Shopify App Store as a drag-and-drop page builder for Shopify merchants. It grew to serve over 200,000 Shopify stores and is one of the most popular page builder apps in the Shopify ecosystem.
PageFly.summary_abuse: PageFly is a legitimate Shopify app for building landing pages and product pages. It has no notable abuse signal; phishing or scam misuse is not a documented pattern for this type of Shopify-native tool.

Pagekit.history: Pagekit is an open-source CMS built with Symfony components and Vue.js, developed by YOOtheme (a German company). Version 1.0 was released in April 2016. Development has stalled and the project is considered abandoned, with YOOtheme redirecting efforts to other products.
Pagekit.summary_abuse: Pagekit CMS has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted open-source CMS with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

PageLayer.history: PageLayer is a WordPress drag-and-drop page builder plugin developed by Softaculous, launched in April 2019. It is distributed as a free GPL plugin on WordPress.org with a premium version available. Softaculous is based in the UAE.
PageLayer.summary_abuse: PageLayer is a self-hosted WordPress plugin with no notable abuse signal. It has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target, and no documented misuse patterns.

Pagely.history: Pagely was founded in 2009 as the first managed WordPress hosting provider, pioneering the managed hosting category. In 2021, it was acquired by GoDaddy and now operates as part of GoDaddy's portfolio of hosting solutions.
Pagely.summary_abuse: Pagely is a managed WordPress hosting service under GoDaddy with no notable abuse signal. As an enterprise-focused hosting provider, it is not a platform typically targeted for phishing or other fraudulent activities.

Pagemaker.history: Pagemaker is an Indian mobile-first landing page builder launched around 2018, targeting performance marketers and small businesses.
Pagemaker.summary_abuse: Pagemaker is a landing page builder that allows users to create hosted pages. Landing page builders are sometimes used to create fake storefronts or phishing landing pages, but Pagemaker is a small vendor without documented widespread abuse incidents.

PageNudge.history: PageNudge is a small website personalization SaaS tool for displaying behavior-triggered messages to visitors.
PageNudge.summary_abuse: PageNudge is a website personalization tool with no notable abuse signal as an attack platform, though behavior-triggered messaging tools can sometimes be configured for manipulative dark patterns.

Pagetiger.history: PageTiger was founded in 2009 in Lincoln, UK, originally as PageTurn Interactive. In 2009 it rebranded to PageTiger after acquiring Communikator Publishing. It has remained an independent UK company focused on digital content creation for organizations.
Pagetiger.summary_abuse: PageTiger is a digital content platform for organizations with no notable abuse signal. It is primarily used by enterprises for internal and marketing communications and has no documented misuse patterns.

PageUp.history: PageUp was founded in 1997 in Australia as a talent management and recruitment software provider. Previously backed by Battery Ventures and Accel-KKR, it was acquired by EQT private equity (BPEA Fund VIII) in October 2024 to accelerate global expansion.
PageUp.summary_abuse: PageUp is an enterprise HR and recruitment SaaS platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves large corporate, university, and public-sector clients and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Pagevamp.history: Pagevamp was founded in 2013 by three University of Pennsylvania students backed by First Round's Dorm Room Fund. It creates websites by pulling content from Facebook and Instagram pages, targeting small businesses.
Pagevamp.summary_abuse: Pagevamp is a small website builder with no notable abuse signal. Like other website builders it could theoretically be used to host fraudulent pages, but there are no documented patterns of misuse.

PagineGialle.history: PagineGialle (Italian Yellow Pages) has roots going back to 1966 as the Italian business directory. The digital platform is operated by ItaliaOnline, which provides website builder and online presence services to Italian businesses.
PagineGialle.summary_abuse: PagineGialle is an Italian business directory and website builder operated by ItaliaOnline. It has no notable abuse signal; it primarily serves Italian SMBs establishing online presence and is not associated with fraudulent use patterns.

Pagolight.history: Pagolight is an Italian Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL) service allowing consumers to pay in installments for online purchases. It operates primarily in the Italian market. No notable ownership changes have been documented.
Pagolight.summary_abuse: Pagolight is a BNPL payment system primarily used in Italy. BNPL services can be associated with fraud and social engineering targeting consumers, but there are no specific documented abuse patterns unique to Pagolight beyond typical payment fraud risks.

PagSeguro.history: PagSeguro was founded in 2006 in Brazil as an online payment service by UOL (Brazilian internet company Universo Online). The company launched an IPO on the NYSE in 2018. It is one of the largest digital payment platforms in Brazil serving millions of merchants and consumers.
PagSeguro.summary_abuse: PagSeguro has no notable abuse signal beyond what is typical for major payment platforms. It is a leading Brazilian payment processor serving legitimate merchants; like all major payment platforms it may be implicated in occasional fraud cases but has compliance infrastructure and PCI DSS certification.

Paidy.history: Paidy was founded in Japan as a BNPL and payments intermediary platform. In September 2021, PayPal acquired Paidy for approximately $2.7 billion, making it one of the largest fintech acquisitions in Japan. It continues to operate under its own brand as a PayPal subsidiary.
Paidy.summary_abuse: Paidy is a Japan-focused BNPL payment service owned by PayPal. BNPL platforms can face fraud risk including account takeover and fraudulent transaction patterns, but Paidy operates in a regulated Japanese market with machine learning-based credit underwriting. No specific major abuse incidents are documented.

Palbin.history: Palbin was founded in 2010 by Alejandro Fanjul and Enrique Andreu in Zaragoza, Spain. It is an ecommerce platform designed for Spanish small businesses to create and manage online stores. It has remained independent with no notable acquisitions.
Palbin.summary_abuse: Palbin is a Spanish ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves small businesses in Spain and has no documented patterns of fraudulent misuse.

Palo Alto Networks - GlobalProtect.history: GlobalProtect is a VPN and network security product from Palo Alto Networks, a publicly traded cybersecurity company (NASDAQ: PANW) founded in 2005. It provides secure remote access to enterprise networks and is widely deployed in large organizations globally.
Palo Alto Networks - GlobalProtect.summary_abuse: GlobalProtect is an enterprise VPN product from Palo Alto Networks. As a security product, it is a target for exploitation rather than a tool for abuse. CVEs have been disclosed in GlobalProtect components (notably CVE-2024-3400 in 2024), but the product itself is not used to conduct abuse. Palo Alto Networks has an active bug bounty and rapid disclosure process.

Paloma.history: Paloma was founded in 2017 by Kelsey Hunter in New York City. It is a VC-backed startup that enables ecommerce businesses to sell directly through messaging channels such as Instagram DMs, with automated personal shopping conversations. It raised $4M in funding.
Paloma.summary_abuse: Paloma is a Shopify app for social commerce messaging with no notable abuse signal. It operates within established platform (Shopify, Instagram) constraints and has no documented patterns of misuse.

Pancake.history: Pancake is a Vietnamese omnichannel business messaging platform founded in 2009 in Hanoi, Vietnam. It serves over 250,000 businesses primarily in Vietnam and Southeast Asia with chat commerce, order management, and CRM features. No notable ownership changes have been documented.
Pancake.summary_abuse: Pancake is a business messaging platform for SMEs in Southeast Asia with no notable abuse signal. While messaging platforms can be misused for spam, there are no documented abuse patterns specific to Pancake.

Panda CSS.history: Panda CSS is a zero-runtime CSS-in-JS framework developed by the Chakra UI team, released in 2023. It generates atomic CSS at build time with TypeScript type safety, aiming to combine the developer experience of CSS-in-JS with the performance of static CSS extraction.
Panda CSS.summary_abuse: Panda CSS has no notable abuse signal. It is a build-time CSS framework with no runtime or consumer-facing surface that would be targeted by fraudsters.

Panda Video.history: Panda Video is a Brazilian video hosting SaaS platform focused on creators and online course providers, launched around 2019.
Panda Video.summary_abuse: Panda Video is a video hosting platform primarily serving online course creators. Video hosting platforms can theoretically be used to distribute pirated content, but Panda Video is a small commercial service with no documented significant abuse incidents.

Pandectes.history: Pandectes is a Greek-founded privacy compliance SaaS for Shopify, launched around 2021, focused on GDPR and CCPA cookie consent management for ecommerce stores.
Pandectes.summary_abuse: Pandectes is a privacy compliance platform that helps websites satisfy cookie consent requirements. It has no notable abuse signal and by design collects minimal consumer data.

Pandectes GDPR Compliance.history: Pandectes GDPR Compliance is a Shopify-focused cookie consent and privacy compliance product from Pandectes, a Greek company launched around 2021. It is the Shopify App Store variant of the Pandectes platform focused specifically on GDPR and related privacy regulations.
Pandectes GDPR Compliance.summary_abuse: Pandectes GDPR Compliance is a privacy consent management tool with no notable abuse signal. It processes consent data rather than consumer financial or personal data in ways that attract fraudsters.

Panelbear.history: Panelbear was founded in 2020 by Anthony Najjar Simon in Munich, Germany, as a privacy-first web analytics and performance monitoring tool designed for developers. It operates as an indie/solo project with no institutional funding.
Panelbear.summary_abuse: Panelbear has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend analytics tool with no consumer-facing content hosting or URL shortening features, and its privacy-focused design minimises data collection that could be misused.

Pannellum.history: Pannellum is an open-source panorama viewer for the web created by Matthew Petroff around 2013. MIT-licensed with no backing company.
Pannellum.summary_abuse: Pannellum is a client-side panorama rendering library with no consumer-facing hosting surface and no abuse signal.

Pantheon.history: Pantheon was founded in 2010 in San Francisco as a managed hosting and WebOps platform for WordPress and Drupal. It has raised significant VC funding and grown to host hundreds of thousands of sites for agencies and enterprises. No major acquisitions or rebrands are documented.
Pantheon.summary_abuse: Pantheon is a managed hosting platform for WordPress and Drupal used primarily by agencies and enterprises. While any hosting platform can be used to serve malicious content, Pantheon enforces terms of service and has an active security team. Its focus on agency and enterprise customers with paid plans reduces abuse risk compared to free-tier hosting services. No notable abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Papa Parse.history: Papa Parse was created by Matt Holt and first released in 2013 as the fastest in-browser CSV parser for JavaScript. It became the de facto standard for client-side CSV parsing.
Papa Parse.summary_abuse: Papa Parse is a client-side CSV parsing library with no server-side or hosted surface and no abuse signal.

papaya CMS.history: papaya CMS is an open-source PHP-based content management system developed in Germany, primarily used by German-speaking organizations. The project has been largely dormant in recent years.
papaya CMS.summary_abuse: papaya CMS is a niche open-source CMS with no notable abuse signal documented.

Paper.js.history: Paper.js is an open-source vector graphics scripting framework created by Juerg Lehni and Jonathan Puckey, released in 2011. It is built on the HTML5 Canvas and MIT-licensed, with no backing company.
Paper.js.summary_abuse: Paper.js is a client-side vector graphics library with no server-side hosting surface and no abuse signal.

Paperless Pipeline.history: Paperless Pipeline is a US-based real estate transaction management SaaS founded around 2008, serving real estate brokerages for document management and commission tracking.
Paperless Pipeline.summary_abuse: Paperless Pipeline is a real estate document management platform with no notable abuse signal. It is used exclusively by real estate professionals in a B2B context.

Papirfly.history: Papirfly is a Norwegian brand management and marketing asset creation platform founded around 2000, serving large enterprise and retail brands globally with centralized brand compliance tools.
Papirfly.summary_abuse: Papirfly is an enterprise brand management platform with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

Paradox.history: Paradox was founded in 2016 in Scottsdale, Arizona as an AI-driven recruiting assistant company. Its primary product Olivia is an AI chatbot for HR teams. It has raised significant VC funding and grown to serve large enterprise clients across multiple industries.
Paradox.summary_abuse: Paradox has no notable abuse signal. It is an AI recruiting SaaS used by HR departments at enterprises, with no consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous access features exploitable for fraud.

Paraglide JS.history: Paraglide JS is a compiler-based i18n JavaScript library developed by the inlang project team, first released in 2023. It generates tree-shakable message functions for frontend localization, targeting modern JavaScript frameworks.
Paraglide JS.summary_abuse: Paraglide JS is a client-side i18n library with no server-side hosted surface and no abuse signal.

parallax.js.history: parallax.js is a small JavaScript library for creating parallax scrolling effects, created around 2014. No notable ownership or deprecation events.
parallax.js.summary_abuse: parallax.js has no notable abuse signal; it is a small client-side visual effect library.

parcel.history: Parcel was created by Devon Govett in 2017 as a zero-configuration JavaScript application bundler, competing with webpack. It gained significant adoption for its ease of use and is maintained by an active open-source community.
parcel.summary_abuse: parcel has no notable abuse signal; it is a build tool used by developers with no consumer-facing surface.

Parcelforce.history: Parcelforce is the UK parcel courier division of Royal Mail, established in 1990 as a distinct business unit from the broader postal service. It is a subsidiary of Royal Mail Group and operates as one of the major UK parcel delivery services.
Parcelforce.summary_abuse: Parcelforce, as with major courier brands, is occasionally impersonated in phishing and smishing campaigns where criminals send fake delivery-failure texts or emails to steal credentials or payments. This is brand abuse by third parties rather than any platform vulnerability. Parcelforce itself is a legitimate established carrier with no known institutional exploitation.

ParcelPanel.history: ParcelPanel is a Chinese ecommerce order tracking and returns management SaaS launched around 2019, primarily targeting Shopify and WooCommerce merchants.
ParcelPanel.summary_abuse: ParcelPanel is an order tracking platform. Shipping tracking pages can be used as phishing lures impersonating delivery notifications, but ParcelPanel as a platform has no documented systematic abuse as attack infrastructure.

Parentapps.history: Parentapps is a UK school communication SaaS platform providing parent-teacher messaging and school update tools, serving UK primary and secondary schools.
Parentapps.summary_abuse: Parentapps is a school communication platform serving verified educational institutions in the UK. It has no consumer-facing free sign-up and no notable abuse signal.

ParkFlow.history: ParkFlow is a SaaS platform for managing airport parking bookings. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
ParkFlow.summary_abuse: ParkFlow is a niche B2B airport parking management SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

ParkingCrew.history: ParkingCrew is a domain parking and monetization service founded around 2009 in Germany, operated as part of the Team Internet group (formerly United Internet Domain Services). It monetizes parked and expired domains through pay-per-click advertising.
ParkingCrew.summary_abuse: ParkingCrew monetizes parked domains with pay-per-click ads, which are commonly used to serve deceptive or malicious advertisements. Parked domain services have a documented history of serving malvertising, typosquatting content, and misleading ads on domains resembling legitimate brands. While ParkingCrew itself does not provide the fraudulent content, its infrastructure is routinely seen in brand impersonation and phishing-adjacent contexts due to the nature of domain parking.

Parmin Cloud.history: Parmin Cloud is an Iranian IaaS cloud services provider. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Parmin Cloud.summary_abuse: Parmin Cloud is an Iranian IaaS provider. Iranian cloud hosting carries elevated potential for sanctions-circumvention use cases, and Iranian infrastructure is sometimes used in threat actor campaigns. No specific documented abuse incidents are known for this provider.

Pars Elecom Portal.history: Pars Elecom Portal is an Iranian ASP.NET-based CMS and web portal platform, primarily targeting Persian-speaking businesses and organizations.
Pars Elecom Portal.summary_abuse: Pars Elecom Portal is a regional Iranian CMS with no notable abuse signal documented outside its primary market.

Parse Platform.history: Parse Platform was originally created by Ilya Sukhar in 2011 and acquired by Facebook in 2013. Facebook shut down the hosted Parse service in January 2017, after which the core team open-sourced the platform. The Parse community has maintained and extended it since under a community-driven governance model, with significant adoption in mobile app backends.
Parse Platform.summary_abuse: Parse Platform is an open-source backend framework with no notable direct abuse signal. As a self-hosted backend, it does not expose a consumer surface that attackers target. Misconfigured open Parse instances have been highlighted in security research as exposing data, but this is a deployment issue rather than intrinsic platform abuse.

Parse.ly.history: Parse.ly was founded in 2009 by Sachin Kamdar and Andrew Montalenti as a content analytics platform for digital publishers. It raised $12.9 million in VC funding. In February 2021, WordPress VIP, a division of Automattic, acquired Parse.ly to integrate its analytics capabilities into the enterprise WordPress offering.
Parse.ly.summary_abuse: Parse.ly is a content analytics platform acquired by Automattic. It embeds tracking scripts on publisher websites to measure content performance, which is a standard practice. No notable abuse incidents or misuse of the platform are publicly documented.

PARSICO.history: PARSICO is an Iranian website builder and CMS platform offering drag-and-drop site creation tools, primarily targeting Persian-speaking markets.
PARSICO.summary_abuse: PARSICO is a small regional website builder primarily serving Persian-language markets with no notable abuse signal.

Parsley.js.history: Parsley.js is an open-source JavaScript form validation library created by Guillaume Potier, first released around 2012. It became widely adopted for client-side form validation in web applications.
Parsley.js.summary_abuse: Parsley.js is a client-side form validation library with no server-side surface and no abuse signal.

Partial.ly.history: Partial.ly is a US-based payment plan SaaS launched around 2015, enabling merchants to offer installment and deferred payment plans to their customers.
Partial.ly.summary_abuse: Partial.ly is a payment plan processor. BNPL and payment plan platforms see some fraud in the form of friendly-fraud chargebacks, but Partial.ly is a niche B2B tool without a documented fraud-infrastructure abuse profile.

particles.js.history: particles.js was created by Vincent Garreau in 2015 as a lightweight JavaScript library for creating animated particle backgrounds. It became popular for website visual effects and has been largely succeeded by tsParticles.
particles.js.summary_abuse: particles.js has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side visual animation library.

ParticularAudience.history: ParticularAudience is an Australian AI-powered ecommerce personalization platform founded around 2016, focusing on privacy-preserving product recommendation and search personalization for retailers.
ParticularAudience.summary_abuse: ParticularAudience is an enterprise AI personalization platform with no notable abuse signal.

Partner Driven.history: Partner Driven is a US real estate investment partnership program offering coaching and partnership models for property investors.
Partner Driven.summary_abuse: Partner Driven is a real estate investment coaching and partnership service. High-pressure investment coaching programs can involve aggressive marketing, but there are no documented specific fraud incidents involving this vendor's technology.

Partner Fleet.history: Partner Fleet is a small SaaS platform launched around 2021 for building embeddable app marketplace pages, targeting B2B SaaS companies that want to showcase integrations.
Partner Fleet.summary_abuse: Partner Fleet is a B2B app marketplace builder with no notable abuse signal.

Partnerize.history: Partnerize (formerly Performance Horizon) was founded in 2010 in the UK, pivoting to affiliate and partnership management for large brands. It rebranded to Partnerize in 2018 and is backed by institutional investors including Serent Capital.
Partnerize.summary_abuse: Partnerize is an affiliate and partnership management network. Affiliate marketing networks are sometimes exploited by fraudulent affiliates using click fraud, fake lead generation, or cookie stuffing to claim commissions. Partnerize has fraud detection features and publishes an anti-fraud policy, but affiliate networks inherently carry elevated abuse risk compared to purely B2B platforms.

Partnero.history: Partnero is a Lithuanian affiliate and partnership management SaaS launched around 2021, targeting SaaS businesses wanting to run their own affiliate programs.
Partnero.summary_abuse: Partnero is an affiliate program management tool. Affiliate programs can be exploited by fraudulent affiliates, but Partnero is a small B2B platform without documented widespread abuse incidents.

PartnerStack.history: PartnerStack was founded in 2015 in Toronto, Canada as a partner relationship management and affiliate platform for SaaS companies. It has raised significant VC funding and grown to manage partnerships for hundreds of B2B SaaS brands.
PartnerStack.summary_abuse: PartnerStack has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B partner management SaaS used by software companies to manage resellers and affiliates, with no consumer-facing anonymous hosting.

Parts Square.history: Parts Square is an automotive ecommerce platform designed for dealers and distributors selling auto parts online, providing SEO-focused product catalog management.
Parts Square.summary_abuse: Parts Square is an automotive parts ecommerce platform. It has no notable abuse signal as an attack platform, though automotive parts marketplaces occasionally see counterfeit parts listings.

Parttrap ONE.history: Parttrap ONE is a Swedish enterprise ecommerce and PIM (Product Information Management) platform, developed by Parttrap, a company serving primarily industrial and B2B distributors since the 2000s.
Parttrap ONE.summary_abuse: Parttrap ONE is a B2B enterprise ecommerce and PIM platform with no notable abuse signal.

Partytown.history: Partytown was created by Adam Bradley at Builder.io and released in 2021. It is an open-source JavaScript library that relocates resource-intensive third-party scripts to a web worker to improve page performance.
Partytown.summary_abuse: Partytown is a client-side performance library with no server-side surface and no abuse signal.

Passage.history: Passage is a passkey and biometric authentication SaaS product created by 1Password, launched around 2021. It enables developers to implement passwordless authentication via WebAuthn/passkeys and is backed by 1Password, a well-established password manager company.
Passage.summary_abuse: Passage has no notable abuse signal. It is an authentication infrastructure service backed by 1Password, with strong security practices and no consumer-facing hosting or anonymous access features.

Passle.history: Passle is a UK B2B thought leadership and content marketing platform founded around 2012, enabling professionals such as lawyers and consultants to publish expert-led content.
Passle.summary_abuse: Passle is a professional B2B content marketing platform with no notable abuse signal.

Passport.js.history: Passport.js was created by Jared Hanson and first released in 2011. It became the de facto authentication middleware for Node.js with support for over 500 authentication strategies.
Passport.js.summary_abuse: Passport.js is an authentication middleware library with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal. Misconfigured authentication implementations using Passport can create vulnerabilities, but this is a developer implementation issue rather than library abuse.

PathFactory.history: PathFactory (formerly LookBookHQ) was founded in 2012 in Toronto, Canada and rebranded to PathFactory in 2018. It is a B2B content intelligence platform that uses AI to personalize content journeys for buyers, backed by venture capital including investors such as Salesforce Ventures.
PathFactory.summary_abuse: PathFactory is a B2B content personalization platform with no notable abuse signal.

Pathful.history: Pathful is a small website analytics and conversion optimization SaaS tool with no notable public history documented.
Pathful.summary_abuse: Pathful is a small website analytics tool with no notable abuse signal.

Paths.js.history: Paths.js is an open-source JavaScript library for generating SVG paths for charts and data visualisation, created by Andrea Ferretti around 2013. It appears to be in low-activity maintenance mode.
Paths.js.summary_abuse: Paths.js is a client-side SVG path generation library with no server-side surface and no abuse signal.

Patient Prism.history: Patient Prism is a US dental practice analytics and AI call coaching platform founded around 2016, helping dental offices improve appointment booking conversion rates.
Patient Prism.summary_abuse: Patient Prism is a dental practice analytics platform handling patient call data. As a HIPAA-compliant platform used in healthcare settings, it has no notable abuse signal.

PatientLoop.history: PatientLoop is a small UK SaaS analytics platform for measuring service impact and ROI, primarily targeting healthcare and social care organizations.
PatientLoop.summary_abuse: PatientLoop is a B2B analytics platform for health services with no notable abuse signal.

PatientSites.history: PatientSites is a US provider of websites and lead generation for physical therapy practices, offering templated website solutions and digital marketing services for PT clinics.
PatientSites.summary_abuse: PatientSites is a healthcare practice website builder with no notable abuse signal.

Patreon.history: Patreon was founded in 2013 by musician Jack Conte and Sam Yam in San Francisco as a membership platform enabling creators to receive recurring payments from fans. It has raised over $400 million in VC funding and grew to host hundreds of thousands of creators.
Patreon.summary_abuse: Patreon is occasionally used for scam creator accounts that solicit pledges without delivering content, or to collect funds under a fraudulent creator identity. The platform has content policies and takedown mechanisms, but the free sign-up and custom page creation makes low-effort fraud possible. Patreon has been responsive to NSFW-content policy enforcement, with several high-profile enforcement actions over the years. Overall abuse is a small fraction of legitimate creator use.

Pattern by Etsy.history: Pattern is an Etsy product launched in 2016 that allows Etsy sellers to create standalone ecommerce websites integrated with their Etsy shop inventory. It is a subsidiary product of Etsy, a publicly traded company.
Pattern by Etsy.summary_abuse: Pattern by Etsy has minimal abuse risk as it is tied directly to verified Etsy seller accounts. Fraud on the platform would be handled through Etsy policies and enforcement. No notable abuse incidents specific to Pattern are documented.

Pay It Later.history: Pay It Later is an Australian buy-now-pay-later service, primarily operating in the Australian ecommerce market. It offers weekly installment payments for online purchases.
Pay It Later.summary_abuse: Pay It Later is an Australian BNPL payment service. BNPL services are susceptible to identity fraud and account takeover, where fraudsters use stolen identities to make purchases with deferred payment. Legitimate use predominates but the service has standard BNPL fraud exposure.

Pay..history: Pay. (pay.nl) is a Dutch payment service provider founded around 2010, offering online and in-store payment processing primarily for Dutch and European merchants.
Pay..summary_abuse: Pay. is a Dutch payment processor regulated under PSD2 with standard anti-fraud controls. Payment processors see some fraud but Pay. is a regulated financial services provider with standard risk management.

PayBright.history: PayBright was a Canadian buy now pay later fintech company founded around 2009. In January 2021, Affirm acquired PayBright for approximately CAD $340 million, expanding its BNPL presence into Canada. PayBright continues to operate as an Affirm subsidiary.
PayBright.summary_abuse: PayBright is a BNPL platform acquired by Affirm. Like all BNPL services, it faces industry-wide payment fraud risks. As a subsidiary of publicly traded Affirm, it operates under strong compliance and fraud detection requirements. No platform-specific abuse incidents are prominently documented.

Paycove.history: Paycove is a small revenue operations SaaS platform for unified payment, billing, and financial reporting, launched around 2020.
Paycove.summary_abuse: Paycove is a B2B revenue operations platform with no notable abuse signal.

Paydock.history: Paydock is an Australian payment orchestration platform founded around 2014, enabling merchants to connect and manage multiple payment gateways and services through a unified API.
Paydock.summary_abuse: Paydock is a payment orchestration platform for enterprise merchants with standard PCI-DSS compliance. It has no notable specific abuse signal beyond standard payment processor fraud exposure.

PayFast.history: PayFast was founded in 2007 as one of South Africa earliest online payment gateways. It is widely used by South African ecommerce merchants and non-profits. No notable acquisitions are documented.
PayFast.summary_abuse: PayFast is a South African payment gateway with standard fraud exposure typical of payment processors. It operates under South African regulatory oversight and PCI DSS standards. No platform-specific abuse incidents are prominently documented.

Payflex.history: Payflex is a South African BNPL service founded around 2017, offering interest-free 6-week installments for South African online shoppers. It was acquired by Woolworths Financial Services, a subsidiary of Woolworths Holdings.
Payflex.summary_abuse: Payflex is a South African BNPL service regulated as a financial services provider. BNPL services face identity fraud and account takeover risks, and Payflex operates in the relatively high-fraud South African ecommerce environment, though it has standard financial-services fraud controls.

PayGreen.history: PayGreen is a French payment processor founded around 2011, focusing on sustainable payment solutions and offering multi-payment functionality including installments and donations alongside standard payment processing.
PayGreen.summary_abuse: PayGreen is a French payment processor regulated under EU financial services law. Standard payment processors see fraud attempts but PayGreen has standard PCI-DSS compliance and fraud controls. No notable specific abuse incidents have been documented.

PayHere.history: PayHere is a UK-based payment link platform founded around 2018, enabling small businesses and freelancers to collect payments via shareable links without a full ecommerce setup.
PayHere.summary_abuse: PayHere is a payment link platform with a free tier allowing quick setup of payment collection pages. Payment link tools with easy signup can be used in scams where fraudsters send fake payment requests to victims. PayHere has standard merchant vetting but the ease of setup creates some inherent risk.

Payhip.history: Payhip was founded in 2013 in the UK as a platform for creators to sell digital products, e-books, and memberships with no upfront costs, taking a commission on sales.
Payhip.summary_abuse: Payhip allows anyone to create a storefront and sell digital products with a free tier and minimal verification. This has been exploited to sell pirated e-books, unlicensed digital content, and fraudulent courses. The platform takes a commission on sales which provides some incentive for enforcement, but the ease of signup and free hosting of downloadable files makes it a recurring target for digital piracy and misleading product listings.

PayJustNow.history: PayJustNow is a South African buy-now-pay-later service founded around 2018, offering three-installment payment splits for South African ecommerce merchants.
PayJustNow.summary_abuse: PayJustNow is a South African BNPL service with standard identity verification and fraud controls for financial services. BNPL services are susceptible to identity fraud, but PayJustNow operates in a regulated financial services context.

PayKickStart.history: PayKickstart is a US shopping cart and affiliate management SaaS founded around 2014, targeting digital product sellers and info-marketers with checkout optimization and affiliate program management.
PayKickStart.summary_abuse: PayKickstart is a checkout and affiliate platform used heavily in the digital marketing and info-product space. This niche is associated with high-pressure sales tactics and occasionally deceptive upsells. Affiliate programs managed through PayKickstart can be exploited by fraudulent affiliates using spam or misleading promotions. The platform has some documented use in aggressively marketed digital products.

Payl8r.history: Payl8r is a UK BNPL and online finance platform founded around 2014, offering repayment plans for online purchases at UK merchants.
Payl8r.summary_abuse: Payl8r is a UK BNPL financial service regulated by the FCA. It faces standard BNPL fraud risks including identity fraud and credit abuse, and is subject to UK consumer credit regulation.

Payload CMS.history: Payload CMS is a headless JavaScript/TypeScript CMS created by James Mikrut and released in 2021. It has rapidly gained traction in the developer community as a modern, code-first alternative to traditional CMS platforms. The company raised VC funding and has grown to significant npm download numbers.
Payload CMS.summary_abuse: Payload CMS is an open-source headless CMS that is self-hosted and has no consumer-facing surface exploitable for fraud. No abuse signal is documented.

Paylocity.history: Paylocity was founded in 1997 by Steve Sarowitz in Chicago as a payroll software company. It went public on NASDAQ in 2014 and has grown into one of the leading cloud-based HCM and payroll platforms for mid-market US businesses.
Paylocity.summary_abuse: Paylocity is a publicly traded US HR and payroll SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It handles employee payroll data under strict US regulatory requirements. No platform-specific abuse incidents are documented.

Paymattic.history: Paymattic is a WordPress payment plugin from WPManageNinja, a Bangladeshi WordPress plugin company, launched around 2020 to provide payment collection and donation features for WordPress sites.
Paymattic.summary_abuse: Paymattic is a WordPress payment collection plugin. WordPress payment plugins require careful implementation to avoid vulnerabilities, but Paymattic has no documented specific widespread abuse incidents.

Paymenter.history: Paymenter is an open-source billing and webshop platform for hosting companies, first published on GitHub in October 2022 under the MIT license and built with Laravel. It has grown to approximately 1,700 GitHub stars as a community-maintained project with no institutional backing.
Paymenter.summary_abuse: Paymenter is a self-hosted open-source billing tool for hosting companies. Being self-hosted and requiring technical setup, it is not a turnkey platform that low-sophistication fraudsters would deploy. No public reports of the project being used in abusive hosting infrastructure were found.

Paymentus.history: Paymentus was founded in 2004 and is a US electronic billing and payment platform serving utilities, government, and insurance. It went public on NYSE in 2021 and serves hundreds of millions of bill payments annually.
Paymentus.summary_abuse: Paymentus is an enterprise bill payment platform used by utilities and government entities with strong compliance and fraud controls. No notable platform-specific abuse incidents are documented.

Payoneer.history: Payoneer was founded in 2005 in New York as a cross-border payments platform for freelancers and digital businesses. It went public on NASDAQ via SPAC merger in 2021 under the ticker PAYO. It serves millions of users globally for marketplace payouts and international business payments.
Payoneer.summary_abuse: Payoneer is a mainstream cross-border payments platform used legitimately by millions of freelancers and businesses. However, it has been implicated in fraud schemes where scammers offer fake job opportunities and request Payoneer account setups, and the platform has appeared in romance scam and advance-fee fraud flows. Payoneer has anti-fraud programs and KYC processes, but as a high-volume international money transfer service it faces persistent fraud exploitation. The vendor has responded to regulatory and fraud concerns over the years.

PayPal.history: PayPal was founded in 1998 by Max Levchin, Peter Thiel, Luke Nosek, and Ken Howery. It was acquired by eBay in 2002 for $1.5 billion and spun off as an independent public company again in 2015. It is among the largest digital payment platforms globally with hundreds of millions of active accounts.
PayPal.summary_abuse: PayPal is one of the most impersonated brands in phishing campaigns globally, with attackers sending fake payment receipts or account alerts to steal credentials. PayPal accounts are also a frequent target for account takeover and money mule recruitment. As a dominant platform, PayPal has sophisticated fraud detection, active enforcement, and a bug bounty program. The abuse rate relative to total transaction volume is low, but the absolute volume of PayPal-related fraud incidents is among the highest of any payment platform.

PayPal Credit.history: PayPal Credit is a revolving credit line product offered by PayPal (formerly BillMeLater before PayPal's acquisition of it in 2008), allowing consumers to defer payment for purchases made through PayPal. It is a financial product of PayPal, the publicly traded company.
PayPal Credit.summary_abuse: PayPal Credit faces the same abuse landscape as PayPal broadly, including phishing emails impersonating PayPal Credit notifications and account takeover attempts. As a credit product it is also subject to credit fraud using stolen identities. PayPal manages this with robust fraud detection as part of its broader platform.

PayPal Marketing Solutions.history: PayPal Marketing Solutions is a merchant intelligence and shopper insights product of PayPal, giving merchants access to shopper behavioral data to improve conversion. It was launched as part of PayPal commerce platform expansion.
PayPal Marketing Solutions.summary_abuse: PayPal Marketing Solutions collects cross-merchant shopping behaviour data to provide merchants with audience insights. Its heavy data collection raises privacy concerns, but as a PayPal product it operates under established privacy policies. No platform-specific abuse incidents are documented.

Payplug.history: Payplug was founded in 2012 in France as a payment gateway specifically for French SMEs. It was acquired by Crédit Mutuel Arkéa, a major French banking group, which invested in the company to offer payment services to its SME banking clients.
Payplug.summary_abuse: Payplug is a French payment processor regulated under PSD2 with PCI-DSS compliance and standard anti-fraud controls. It operates primarily in the French SME market and has no notable specific abuse incidents documented.

Payrexx.history: Payrexx is a Swiss online payment platform founded around 2013, serving Swiss and European merchants with payment page creation and payment link tools compliant with Swiss and EU regulations.
Payrexx.summary_abuse: Payrexx is a Swiss payment platform operating under EU and Swiss financial regulations with standard PCI-DSS compliance. It has no notable specific abuse incidents documented.

Paysafe.history: Paysafe was founded in 1996 in the UK and grew through multiple acquisitions, including Skrill, NETELLER, and income Access. It was taken private by Blackstone and CVC in 2017 and relisted on the NYSE via SPAC in 2020. It serves online gambling, gaming, and mainstream commerce.
Paysafe.summary_abuse: Paysafe is a major global payments platform heavily used in online gambling and gaming, sectors with elevated fraud risk. Its Paysafecard prepaid product has historically appeared in fraud scenarios including premium-rate SMS scams and advance-fee fraud, as prepaid products allow anonymous or pseudonymous transactions. Paysafe has fraud controls and AML programs but the prepaid and digital wallet segments have documented misuse by bad actors.

Paysera.history: Paysera was founded in 2004 in Lithuania as one of the first European electronic money institutions, offering payment and money transfer services across the EU. It holds an EMI license from the Bank of Lithuania and serves millions of users in the Baltics and Eastern Europe.
Paysera.summary_abuse: Paysera is a licensed EU electronic money institution. As a money transfer service, it has some exposure to money mule fraud and fraudulent merchant account abuse, but it operates under regulatory supervision and has standard AML and KYC controls. No major specific documented incidents distinguish it from other licensed payment providers.

PAYTR.history: PAYTR is a Turkish payment gateway and processing platform founded around 2011, enabling Turkish merchants to accept online payments securely. It is a leading payment infrastructure provider in the Turkish market.
PAYTR.summary_abuse: PAYTR has no notable abuse signal beyond what is typical for any payment processor. It is a Turkish payment gateway used by legitimate online merchants, with PCI DSS compliance as a baseline security control.

PayU Payment.history: PayU was founded in 2002 and is an international payment processor operating across Africa, Asia, Central and Eastern Europe, and Latin America. It is a subsidiary of Prosus, itself a subsidiary of Naspers, a South African multinational. PayU has made multiple acquisitions to expand globally.
PayU Payment.summary_abuse: PayU is an international payment processor with standard payment fraud risk. Operating in emerging markets it faces elevated fraud rates in some jurisdictions, but operates under PCI DSS and regulatory compliance. No platform-specific abuse incidents are prominently documented.

Payva.history: Payva is a Buy Now Pay Later platform specifically designed for the creator economy, enabling structured payment plans for creative work and services. It appears to have launched around 2021 as a niche BNPL provider targeting freelancers and creators.
Payva.summary_abuse: Payva is a niche BNPL service for creators with no notable abuse signal in public reporting. As with all BNPL platforms, there is some risk of fraudulent purchase attempts using stolen payment credentials, but the creator-focused niche limits the attack surface relative to large consumer BNPL providers.

PayWhirl.history: PayWhirl is a US recurring payments and subscription management platform founded around 2012, providing tools for merchants to offer subscription billing and payment plans.
PayWhirl.summary_abuse: PayWhirl is a recurring payment platform. Subscription billing platforms can be misused for subscription traps, but PayWhirl is a B2B tool without a documented significant abuse profile.

PCI Proxy.history: PCI Proxy is a payment tokenization platform built by Datatrans AG, a Swiss payment company. It provides card data proxy and tokenization services that allow businesses to handle payment flows without storing card data in their own environments, reducing PCI DSS scope.
PCI Proxy.summary_abuse: PCI Proxy has no notable abuse signal. It is a payment tokenization and proxy service used by businesses to reduce PCI DSS scope, handling cardholder data in a compliant infrastructure that is not accessible to bad actors.

PCRecruiter.history: PCRecruiter is an applicant tracking and CRM platform for recruiting agencies and staffing professionals, developed by Main Sequence Technology Inc. It has been in operation since the late 1990s and is one of the longer-standing ATS/CRM solutions in the staffing industry.
PCRecruiter.summary_abuse: PCRecruiter has no notable abuse signal. It is a recruiting CRM and ATS platform used by staffing agencies with no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract fraud operators.

PDF.js.history: PDF.js is an open-source JavaScript library developed by Mozilla for rendering PDF documents in web browsers using HTML5 canvas. It was first released in 2011 and is the PDF rendering engine built into Firefox. The project is maintained by Mozilla and the open-source community under the Apache 2.0 license.
PDF.js.summary_abuse: PDF.js has no notable abuse signal as a library. It is a foundational browser PDF rendering engine maintained by Mozilla, used by millions of web applications to display PDFs without plugins.

pdfmake.history: pdfmake is a client/server JavaScript library for creating PDF documents, created around 2014. It supports both browser and Node.js environments and is widely used for generating PDF reports.
pdfmake.summary_abuse: pdfmake has no notable abuse signal; it is a PDF generation library with no server-side hosted service component.

Peachie.history: Peachie is an all-in-one LMS and CMS platform for creating and selling online courses, targeted at individual creators and small course businesses. It appears to have launched around 2020 with freemium pricing.
Peachie.summary_abuse: Peachie is a niche LMS/CMS for online course creators with no notable abuse signal. The platform is content-creation focused and does not offer features commonly targeted for phishing or spam abuse.

Peachs.history: Peachs is a Squarespace-focused referral marketing tool that enables ecommerce stores to run word-of-mouth and social sharing campaigns. It is a niche SaaS targeting Squarespace merchants specifically.
Peachs.summary_abuse: Peachs is a referral marketing tool for Squarespace stores with no notable abuse signal. As a marketing platform it collects customer email data for referral programs, but operates within the mainstream ecommerce referral space with no documented fraud or scam patterns.

Pear Commerce.history: Pear Commerce is a US-based retail ecommerce platform founded around 2019 that connects consumer packaged goods (CPG) brands to retailers through shoppable tools and marketing analytics. It targets enterprise CPG brands needing retail media and shoppable experiences.
Pear Commerce.summary_abuse: Pear Commerce is a B2B CPG retail ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It operates in a closed enterprise market connecting CPG brands to retailers, with no consumer-facing public signup or content hosting features relevant to phishing or scam use cases.

PebblePost.history: PebblePost was founded in 2014 in New York by Lewis Gersh and Duane Lempges as a programmatic direct mail retargeting platform, bridging digital intent signals (website visits, abandoned carts) with physical mail delivery to consumer households. The company raised venture funding and positions itself as a premium retargeting channel for mid-to-large ecommerce brands.
PebblePost.summary_abuse: PebblePost is a B2B programmatic direct mail platform that processes online behavioral data to target physical mail. It collects consumer browsing data for retargeting purposes, which is privacy-sensitive, but operates in the legitimate direct marketing space with no notable abuse or fraud signal. As a data-driven marketing company it is subject to CAN-SPAM and similar regulations.

PeckaDesign Publicator.history: PeckaDesign Publicator is a headless ecommerce platform built by Czech web agency PeckaDesign, targeting large enterprise online stores that need high performance and custom modifications. It is a niche Czech market solution.
PeckaDesign Publicator.summary_abuse: PeckaDesign Publicator is an enterprise headless ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves enterprise customers in the Czech market with no consumer-facing self-signup or content hosting features relevant to abuse.

Peddle.history: Peddle is a US-based car buying service founded around 2012 that provides instant cash offers for used, old, or damaged vehicles, connecting consumers with a network of auto buyers and junkyards. The platform streamlines the private car selling process through an online instant-offer model.
Peddle.summary_abuse: Peddle is a consumer car-buying marketplace with modest abuse exposure. The instant-offer model for vehicles could attract title fraud attempts or scam listings, but the platform operates in a regulated space with identity verification for sellers. No notable large-scale abuse campaigns have been publicly documented.

Peecho.history: Peecho was founded around 2011 in the Netherlands as a print-on-demand API and platform enabling publishers and digital content creators to sell their digital publications as printed physical products. It serves a niche B2B segment of publishers and online stores.
Peecho.summary_abuse: Peecho is a print-on-demand B2B platform with no notable abuse signal. It enables publishers to sell digital content as printed products and has no consumer-facing content hosting features that would be targeted for phishing or scam purposes.

Peek.history: Peek was founded in 2012 in San Francisco by Ruzwana Bashir and Oskar Bruening as a travel experiences booking marketplace and operator software platform. The company raised over $216M in venture capital from investors including Jack Dorsey, Eric Schmidt, Goldman Sachs Alternatives, WestCap, and Springcoast Partners. By 2021, it had processed $2B in bookings. In 2025 it raised a further $70M and acquired ACME Ticketing and Connect&GO to expand its ticketing and event management capabilities.
Peek.summary_abuse: Peek is a well-funded legitimate travel experiences booking and operator management platform. No notable abuse history. Online booking platforms can occasionally be targeted by card testing or fraudulent reservations, but Peek processes payments through established gateways and has PCI compliance obligations.

Peel.history: Peel is a French open-source shopping cart system (peel.fr) that dates from the mid-2000s. Despite the upstream description calling it open source, it is primarily associated with a French commercial entity. The project has seen limited activity in recent years and serves a regional French ecommerce market.
Peel.summary_abuse: Peel is a self-hosted open-source shopping cart primarily used in the French market. As a self-hosted solution, abuse depends on the site operator's practices rather than the platform itself; no notable platform-level abuse signal has been documented.

PeerBoard.history: PeerBoard was founded around 2019 as an embeddable community forum platform targeting startups, marketplaces, and small businesses needing plug-and-play discussion forums. It offers a freemium model with a free tier for basic communities.
PeerBoard.summary_abuse: PeerBoard is a community forum SaaS with a free tier, which presents some potential for spam communities or low-quality content sites. Like most forum platforms, it may attract low-quality signups, but operates in the mainstream community software space with standard moderation tools. No notable large-scale abuse patterns have been documented.

Peerius.history: Peerius is a UK-based ecommerce personalization platform founded around 2010 that analyzes visitor behavior to optimize checkout and product recommendations. It serves mid-market ecommerce retailers with behavioral analytics and personalization tooling.
Peerius.summary_abuse: Peerius is a B2B ecommerce personalization and behavioral analytics platform with no notable abuse signal. It collects visitor behavioral data for ecommerce optimization, which is privacy-sensitive, but operates within the mainstream personalization software space with no documented fraud patterns.

PeerJS.history: PeerJS is an open-source JavaScript library wrapping the WebRTC API to simplify peer-to-peer data, audio, and video communication. It was first released around 2013 and is maintained by community contributors on GitHub.
PeerJS.summary_abuse: PeerJS has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend WebRTC library with no hosted consumer surface; it requires integration by developers and has no direct exposure to phishing or fraud patterns.

PeerPal.history: PeerPal is an admissions ambassador platform for higher education institutions that connects prospective students and families with current students and alumni. It was acquired by or merged into Gravyty, a fundraising and alumni engagement platform for nonprofits and universities.
PeerPal.summary_abuse: PeerPal is a higher education admissions platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves universities and educational institutions in a closed B2B context with no public content hosting or anonymous access features relevant to fraud or scam use.

PeerTube.history: PeerTube was created by Framasoft, a French nonprofit, with development starting around 2015 and a public release in 2018. It is a decentralized, federated video hosting platform using ActivityPub and WebTorrent protocols, designed as a privacy-respecting alternative to YouTube.
PeerTube.summary_abuse: PeerTube instances can host arbitrary video content including potentially illegal material, depending on instance moderation. Because it is a self-hosted, federated platform with no central authority, individual instance operators control content moderation, and lax instances have been reported hosting extremist content. The Framasoft-maintained flagship instance enforces content policies, but the federated architecture means abuse response varies by instance.

Peftrust.history: Peftrust is a French SaaS platform founded around 2019 that helps apparel and footwear brands calculate and report environmental scores using the European Product Environmental Footprint (PEF) Methodology. It targets brands needing EU environmental compliance and green claims documentation.
Peftrust.summary_abuse: Peftrust is a niche B2B environmental compliance platform for apparel brands with no abuse signal. It serves a highly specific enterprise compliance use case with no consumer-facing or content-hosting features relevant to fraud or scam activity.

Pegboard7.history: Pegboard7 is an Australian digital platform provider offering ecommerce, marketing, and enterprise web content management software for medium to large businesses. Based on their website (pegboardsoftware.com.au), it serves a regional Australian/New Zealand market with custom CMS and ecommerce solutions.
Pegboard7.summary_abuse: Pegboard7 is a regional Australian enterprise CMS and ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves medium to corporate clients in a closed B2B context with no public self-signup or content hosting features relevant to fraud.

Peity.history: Peity is a small jQuery plugin created by Ben Pickles around 2013 that renders inline SVG mini charts (pie, line, bar) from element content. It has been largely dormant in recent years as the jQuery ecosystem has declined.
Peity.summary_abuse: Peity has no abuse signal. It is a tiny frontend charting library with no hosted service and no consumer-facing surface.

Pelcro.history: Pelcro is a Canadian subscription and membership management platform founded around 2016, providing publishers, media companies, and digital businesses with tools to manage subscriptions, paywalls, and recurring billing. It targets digital publishers needing managed subscription infrastructure.
Pelcro.summary_abuse: Pelcro is a subscription management platform for digital publishers with no notable abuse signal. It processes recurring payment data for legitimate media businesses and operates within standard payment compliance frameworks. No documented fraud or scam patterns have been associated with the platform.

Pelican.history: Pelican is a Python-based static site generator created by Alexis Metaireau and first released around 2010. It supports reStructuredText and Markdown content formats and generates static HTML files, making it popular among developers who prefer Python over Ruby-based alternatives like Jekyll. It remains a community-maintained open-source project.
Pelican.summary_abuse: Pelican is a Python static site generator with no abuse signal. As a local build tool that generates static HTML files, it has no consumer-facing hosted service and therefore no platform surface area relevant to phishing, spam, or fraud.

PencilBlue.history: PencilBlue is an open-source Node.js CMS and blogging platform released around 2014. Development has stalled and the GitHub repository has been largely abandoned, with no significant activity in recent years.
PencilBlue.summary_abuse: PencilBlue has no abuse signal. It is an abandoned self-hosted Node.js CMS with no hosted service surface and no documented abuse history.

Pendo.history: Pendo was founded in 2013 in Raleigh, NC by Todd Olson and others as a product analytics and in-app guidance platform. It raised significant venture funding including a $100M round in 2019, becoming one of the leading product experience platforms for enterprise SaaS companies.
Pendo.summary_abuse: Pendo has no notable abuse signal. It is a product analytics and in-app messaging SaaS used by enterprise software companies, with no consumer-facing hosting surface. It collects user behavioral data within client applications, raising privacy considerations, but it is not a platform associated with phishing or fraud infrastructure.

Pepper Cloud.history: Pepper Cloud is a Singapore-based CRM SaaS founded around 2017, targeting small and medium businesses across Southeast Asia with sales pipeline and customer relationship management tools. It serves a regional SME market with WhatsApp and messaging integrations.
Pepper Cloud.summary_abuse: Pepper Cloud is a B2B CRM platform for small businesses with no notable abuse signal. It operates in the legitimate sales tooling market and has no consumer-facing content-hosting features that would be exploited for phishing or fraud.

Pepperjam.history: Pepperjam was founded in 1999 as an affiliate marketing network and technology platform. It was acquired by GSI Commerce in 2009, then by eBay Enterprise, before being reacquired by management and later merged with Partnerize, a partnership automation company. The Pepperjam brand has been phased out under the Partnerize parent.
Pepperjam.summary_abuse: Pepperjam is an affiliate marketing network with documented affiliate fraud exposure typical of the industry, including commission fraud, coupon abuse, and click fraud from bad-actor affiliates. Affiliate networks inherently face these risks because they allow third-party publishers to drive traffic on behalf of brands. Pepperjam/Partnerize has affiliate quality controls and publisher vetting, but like all large affiliate networks, some portion of traffic from low-quality affiliates represents a recurring risk.

Peraichi.history: Peraichi is a Japanese website and landing page builder founded around 2017, designed to make it easy for businesses and individuals to create marketing pages without coding. It has grown to become a popular no-code website creation tool in the Japanese market.
Peraichi.summary_abuse: Peraichi is a Japanese page builder with a free tier that allows users to create and publish landing pages. Like most page builders with free tiers, it presents some risk for scam landing pages, but operates in the mainstream Japanese market with standard content policies. No specific large-scale abuse campaigns have been publicly documented.

Percona.history: Percona was founded in 2006 by Peter Zaitsev and Vadim Tkachenko as a MySQL consulting and support company. It developed Percona Server for MySQL, a performance-enhanced drop-in replacement, and later expanded to support PostgreSQL and MongoDB with enterprise tooling and managed services.
Percona.summary_abuse: Percona has no abuse signal. It is a backend database software and support company with no consumer-facing service surface; it is used by enterprises and developers running MySQL-compatible workloads.

Percussion.history: Percussion is a US-based enterprise content management system (CMS) provider that has operated since the 1990s, originally known for its Percussion CM1 and Percussion CMS products targeting mid-market and enterprise web publishers. The company rebranded and pivoted focus over the years, serving education, healthcare, and government verticals.
Percussion.summary_abuse: Percussion is an enterprise CMS platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves mid-market and enterprise clients in regulated verticals and does not offer consumer-facing self-signup or anonymous content hosting features relevant to fraud or scam activity.

Percy.history: Percy is a US-based CRM platform for the real estate industry that uses behavioral data to help real estate agents and mortgage loan officers coordinate referrals and client communication. It appears to have launched around 2018 as a niche proptech CRM.
Percy.summary_abuse: Percy is a niche CRM for real estate professionals with no notable abuse signal. It serves licensed agents and loan officers in a professional B2B context with identity requirements, and has no consumer-facing content hosting features relevant to fraud or scam use.

Perfect Corp.history: Founded in 2015 by Alice Chang in Taiwan, Perfect Corp developed AI and AR-powered virtual try-on technology for cosmetics brands. The company raised $130M in funding including a Series C led by Goldman Sachs in 2021, then went public on the NYSE in October 2022 via a SPAC merger under the ticker PERF.
Perfect Corp.summary_abuse: Perfect Corp has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B beauty augmented reality SaaS platform embedded by cosmetics brands for virtual product try-on, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that bad actors would target.

Perfect Storm.history: Perfect Storm is a real estate CRM platform founded by Joshua Smith and co-founders, headquartered in Champaign, Illinois. It was built by realtors for realtors as an all-in-one lead generation and CRM system.
Perfect Storm.summary_abuse: Perfect Storm has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche real estate CRM used by agents and brokers for lead management, with no public-facing hosting features that would attract fraudsters.

PerfectApps Swift.history: PerfectApps Swift is a Shopify speed optimization app built by PerfectApps. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
PerfectApps Swift.summary_abuse: PerfectApps Swift is a Shopify performance optimization app with no documented abuse signal.

PerfectBot.history: PerfectBot is an AI-powered ecommerce chatbot optimizing customer service. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
PerfectBot.summary_abuse: PerfectBot is an AI ecommerce chatbot SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Perfex CRM.history: Perfex CRM was created by MSTdev and first published on CodeCanyon in 2015 as a self-hosted PHP/MySQL CRM for small businesses and freelancers; it uses a one-time purchase model and has grown through CodeCanyon sales with no major ownership changes.
Perfex CRM.summary_abuse: Perfex CRM is self-hosted software with no notable abuse signal for the vendor itself; because deployments are customer-owned, the abuse risk depends on how individual operators configure and secure their instances rather than on the vendor.

Perfmatters.history: Founded in 2017 by brothers Brian Jackson and Brett Jackson, Perfmatters is a self-funded, bootstrapped WordPress performance plugin focused on Google Core Web Vitals optimization. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
Perfmatters.summary_abuse: Perfmatters has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted WordPress plugin installed by site owners on their own infrastructure, with no consumer-facing surface or hosting capabilities that would attract fraudulent use.

Performance Lab.history: Performance Lab is an official WordPress plugin maintained by the WordPress Performance Group, a team of contributors affiliated with WordPress.org and Automattic. It serves as an experimental space for performance features before they are merged into WordPress core. First released in 2021, it remains actively developed.
Performance Lab.summary_abuse: Performance Lab has no notable abuse signal. It is an official WordPress performance plugin with no consumer-facing hosted surface that fraudsters would target.

Performant Translations.history: Performant Translations is an official WordPress plugin from the WordPress Performance Team, first released in 2023. It improves internationalization speed by replacing PHP gettext files with a faster native implementation. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are on record.
Performant Translations.summary_abuse: Performant Translations has no notable abuse signal. It is an official WordPress plugin focused on improving translation performance with no consumer-facing hosted surface.

PerimeterX.history: PerimeterX was founded in 2014 in San Mateo, California, focused on bot mitigation and web application security. In July 2022 it merged with HUMAN Security (formerly White Ops), with HUMAN as the surviving brand. The combined company serves 500+ enterprise customers with over $100M ARR.
PerimeterX.summary_abuse: PerimeterX is a bot mitigation and fraud protection service, not a tool used for abuse. It is deployed by enterprises to detect and block automated threats including credential stuffing and account takeover. No notable abuse of the platform itself is documented.

Periodic.history: Periodic is a white-label scheduling and appointment booking SaaS. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Periodic.summary_abuse: Periodic is a scheduling SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Peripl.history: Peripl is a French cloud software company offering business management tools including accounting, invoicing, payroll, and project management, primarily targeting SMBs in France.
Peripl.summary_abuse: Peripl has no notable abuse signal. It is a business management SaaS platform used by SMBs for internal accounting and HR functions, with no consumer-facing hosting surface relevant to fraudsters.

Perl.history: Perl was created by Larry Wall in 1987, originally designed for text processing. It became dominant in the 1990s for CGI web scripting and system administration. Perl 6 (later renamed Raku) was a complete redesign started in 2000 and finally released in 2015, while Perl 5 remained the active mainstream branch. Perl's usage has declined significantly since the rise of Python and Ruby in the 2000s.
Perl.summary_abuse: Perl has no notable abuse signal as a programming language. It has no hosted consumer surface and is a general-purpose language used legitimately for system administration, text processing, and web scripting.

Permutive.history: Founded in 2014 in London by Joe Root and Tim Spratt via Y Combinator and Entrepreneur First, Permutive built a publisher-first data management platform using edge computing to process audience data on-device without third-party cookies. The company raised $116M total including a $75M Series C from SoftBank Vision Fund 2.
Permutive.summary_abuse: Permutive has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B publisher data platform that processes first-party audience data for media companies. Its privacy-by-design edge computing architecture and enterprise-only client base reduce exposure to fraudulent use.

Persona.ly.history: Founded around 2013 by Ofir Pasternak in Rehovot, Israel, Persona.ly is a mobile programmatic DSP offering performance-driven user acquisition and re-engagement for app developers. The company has offices across Israel, Korea, Russia, Japan, and China.
Persona.ly.summary_abuse: Persona.ly is an ad-tech platform used for mobile user acquisition. As a DSP processing large volumes of mobile ad requests, it operates in a space with elevated fraud risk including ad fraud and invalid traffic, though its primary customers are legitimate app developers seeking user growth. No specific abuse incidents have been publicly documented.

PersonaClick.history: PersonaClick is a SaaS platform providing personalization, product recommendations, and multichannel marketing services, primarily for e-commerce businesses.
PersonaClick.summary_abuse: PersonaClick has no notable abuse signal. It is a personalization and recommendation engine for e-commerce sites with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would typically target.

Personio.summary_abuse: Personio has no notable abuse signal. It is an HR platform handling employee data for SMEs and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters commonly exploit.

Phaser.summary_abuse: Phaser has no notable abuse signal. It is an open-source JavaScript game framework with no hosted infrastructure or consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.
