PHP.history: PHP was created by Rasmus Lerdorf in 1994 as a set of CGI scripts. The Zend Engine was introduced in PHP 4 (2000) by Zeev Suraski and Andi Gutmans, transforming it into a full programming language. PHP 5 (2004) added full OOP support. PHP 7 (2015) delivered major performance improvements, and PHP 8 (2020) introduced JIT compilation. It powers approximately 77% of websites with server-side language detection.
PHP.summary_abuse: PHP itself has no abuse signal as a language; however, PHP-based web applications have historically been a major attack surface. Poorly coded PHP apps are the most commonly exploited web application type for RCE, file inclusion, and SQL injection attacks. PHP's ubiquity means the language appears on abusive infrastructure, but the language itself is neutral.

PHP-Nuke.history: PHP-Nuke is a free open-source CMS for community portals, originally created by Francisco Burzi around 2000. It was widely adopted in the early 2000s for news and community websites but has been largely abandoned as the software became unmaintained and insecure, with countless forks and variants emerging.
PHP-Nuke.summary_abuse: PHP-Nuke is an abandoned legacy CMS with a poor security history. Its codebase has well-documented SQL injection and XSS vulnerabilities that were never fully patched, making surviving installations attractive targets for attackers seeking to compromise servers for spam, malware hosting, or defacement. Sites still running PHP-Nuke should be considered high risk.

phpAlbum.history: phpAlbum is a PHP photo gallery script created around 2003. It has been largely abandoned as cloud photo sharing replaced self-hosted gallery software.
phpAlbum.summary_abuse: phpAlbum has no notable abuse signal; it is a self-hosted photo gallery script.

phpBB.history: phpBB was first released in 2000 by James Atkinson and has become one of the most widely deployed open-source forum systems. It is maintained by the phpBB Group and has a large ecosystem of plugins and themes.
phpBB.summary_abuse: phpBB forums are occasionally exploited for spam posting and malicious link distribution due to unpatched installations, but the project itself is widely used legitimately.

PHPBoost.history: PHPBoost is a French open-source CMS built with PHP, designed for small community websites and portals. It was founded around 2004 and remains maintained by a small volunteer community, primarily serving French-speaking users.
PHPBoost.summary_abuse: PHPBoost has no notable abuse signal. It is an open-source CMS self-hosted on user servers, with no SaaS hosting surface; outdated installations could be exploited like any unmaintained PHP application, but no documented abuse campaigns are specific to PHPBoost.

phpCMS.history: phpCMS is a German open-source PHP content management system developed in the early 2000s; it has been largely abandoned in favor of more modern CMS platforms.
phpCMS.summary_abuse: phpCMS is an abandoned PHP CMS with no notable abuse signal; it has an outdated codebase and no consumer-facing content hosting surface.

PHPDebugBar.history: PHP Debug Bar is an open-source developer tool library for PHP applications, providing a debug toolbar that displays debugging information collected from various data sources including database queries, profiling data, and session information. It was first released around 2013 and is widely used in PHP development.
PHPDebugBar.summary_abuse: PHP Debug Bar has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer debugging library for PHP web applications with no consumer-facing surface; exposing a debug bar on a production site would be a security misconfiguration, but the library itself is not a tool for abuse.

phpDocumentor.history: phpDocumentor is an open-source documentation generator for PHP code, originally created by Joshua Eichorn in 2001. It is a widely used tool in the PHP ecosystem for generating API documentation from docblock comments.
phpDocumentor.summary_abuse: phpDocumentor has no notable abuse signal; it is a developer tool for generating documentation.

PHPFusion.history: PHP-Fusion is an open-source CMS and web application framework written in PHP, founded around 2003 by Nick Jones. It was popular in the early-to-mid 2000s for gaming and community portals but has since been largely superseded by modern CMS platforms like WordPress and Joomla.
PHPFusion.summary_abuse: PHP-Fusion has no notable specific abuse signal beyond the general risk of outdated PHP CMS installations. Like other aging PHP CMS platforms, outdated installations may be exploited for server compromise, but no major documented abuse campaigns specifically target PHP-Fusion infrastructure.

phpMyAdmin.history: phpMyAdmin was created by Tobias Ratschiller in 1998 and has been maintained by the phpMyAdmin team since 2000. It is one of the most widely deployed MySQL administration tools on the web. Exposed phpMyAdmin installations are a frequent target for automated attacks attempting unauthorized database access.
phpMyAdmin.summary_abuse: phpMyAdmin is a frequent target for automated scanning and brute-force attacks because exposed instances provide direct database access. Misconfigured or default installations have historically been exploited to gain unauthorized database access, drop databases, or inject malicious content. Users should never expose phpMyAdmin to the internet without authentication.

phpPgAdmin.history: phpPgAdmin is a web-based PostgreSQL administration tool created around 2002. It is similar in purpose to phpMyAdmin but for PostgreSQL databases.
phpPgAdmin.summary_abuse: phpPgAdmin has no notable abuse signal; it is a database administration tool that is rarely exposed to the public internet in legitimate deployments.

phpRS.history: phpRS is a Czech CMS written in PHP, primarily used in Czech-speaking countries. The project is largely abandoned.
phpRS.summary_abuse: phpRS has no notable abuse signal; it is a niche CMS mainly used in Czech markets.

phpSQLiteCMS.summary_abuse: phpSQLiteCMS has no notable abuse signal. It is a lightweight self-hosted CMS with no hosted service surface and no documented abuse history.

phpwcms.summary_abuse: phpwcms has no notable abuse signal; it is a niche self-hosted CMS.

Phusion Passenger.summary_abuse: Phusion Passenger has no notable abuse signal. It is a server-side web application server with no consumer-facing hosted surface that fraudsters would target.

pinoox.history: Pinoox is a PHP web framework developed primarily for the Persian/Iranian market. It is a small open-source project with a limited international footprint.
pinoox.summary_abuse: Pinoox has no notable documented abuse signal; it is a niche PHP web framework.

Pinpoll.history: Pinpoll was founded in 2015 in Linz, Austria, as an interactive engagement and data management platform for websites, offering polls, quizzes, and analytics tools.
Pinpoll.summary_abuse: Pinpoll is a website engagement tool for polls and analytics with no notable abuse signal; it collects user interaction data on behalf of site operators and does not provide features commonly exploited for phishing or fraud.

Pinterest.history: Pinterest was founded in 2010 by Ben Silbermann, Paul Sciarra, and Evan Sharp in San Francisco. It launched publicly in 2011 and became one of the fastest-growing social networks. Pinterest went public on the New York Stock Exchange in April 2019 under ticker PINS, with revenue reaching $4.2B by 2025.
Pinterest.summary_abuse: Pinterest has moderate abuse signal as a major social platform. It is used for spam link distribution and occasionally for phishing or counterfeit product promotion via Pins. The platform has trust and safety teams and standard content moderation but its open linking model allows some abuse. Fake brand accounts and affiliate spam are documented issues. Legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates.

Pinterest Ads.history: Pinterest Ads is the advertising platform of Pinterest, Inc., which was founded in 2010 and went public in 2019. Pinterest Ads launched its self-serve advertising system around 2013-2014, growing into a significant ad network for consumer brands.
Pinterest Ads.summary_abuse: Pinterest Ads has low-moderate abuse signal. As an ad platform it can be exploited by fraudsters running counterfeit product ads or scam promotions, which is common across social media ad platforms. Pinterest has ad review policies in place, but no major abuse-specific incidents are documented beyond typical ad fraud patterns seen across the industry.

Pinterest Conversion Tag.history: The Pinterest Conversion Tag is a tracking pixel from Pinterest, Inc. (founded 2010, NYSE:PINS). It enables advertisers to track user actions on their websites after viewing Pinterest ads, introduced alongside Pinterest's advertising platform expansion around 2013-2014.
Pinterest Conversion Tag.summary_abuse: The Pinterest Conversion Tag has low abuse signal. It is a standard ad tracking pixel that collects behavioral data. Like all ad pixels it contributes to cross-site tracking but is not a vector for direct abuse.

Pipedrive.history: Pipedrive was founded in 2010 in Tallinn, Estonia by Timo Rein, Urmas Purde, Ragnar Sass, Martin Henk, and Martin Tajur. In November 2020, Vista Equity Partners acquired a majority stake valuing the company at approximately $1.5 billion, establishing it as a unicorn. The company serves over 100,000 customers globally.
Pipedrive.summary_abuse: Pipedrive has no notable abuse signal. It is a cloud-based sales CRM for businesses with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Piperun.history: Piperun is a Brazilian CRM platform focused on managing complex B2B sales processes and channel partnerships, founded around 2015. It is primarily used by Brazilian companies and has no notable acquisitions on record.
Piperun.summary_abuse: Piperun has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B CRM platform with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

pirobase CMS.history: pirobase CMS was founded in Cologne, Germany in 1995 as imperia, one of the earliest enterprise CMS systems. The company went through various acquisitions and rebrands, becoming pirobase imperia GmbH by 2015. In 2022, the company was acquired by a Swiss IT company.
pirobase CMS.summary_abuse: pirobase CMS is an enterprise content management system used by large organizations and public administrations in German-speaking countries. It has no notable abuse signal.

Piwigo.history: Piwigo is a French open-source photo gallery software that originated as phpWebGallery in 2002. It was renamed Piwigo in 2009 and continues active development. A hosted SaaS version is available alongside the self-hosted option.
Piwigo.summary_abuse: Piwigo has no notable abuse signal. It is an open-source photo gallery platform with minimal consumer-facing hosted surface. Self-hosted instances are under the control of the site owner.

Piwik PRO Core.history: Piwik PRO was founded in 2013 in Krakow, Poland, as a commercial spin-off of the open-source Piwik (later Matomo) analytics project, targeting enterprises requiring privacy-first analytics with on-premise hosting. The company launched the Core free plan in 2022 as a Google Analytics alternative after the US court rulings on Google Analytics GDPR concerns, but discontinued the free tier in late 2025 in favor of paid Business and Enterprise plans. Piwik PRO has held ISO 27001 certification since 2018 and achieved SOC 2 Type II attestation.
Piwik PRO Core.summary_abuse: Piwik PRO Core is a privacy-focused analytics platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a backend analytics tool used by organizations to track their own web traffic and does not provide features that would attract phishing kit operators or scammers. The platform actively positions itself on compliance and data privacy, publishes an acceptable use policy, and its architecture restricts data hosting to specific cloud regions with contractual safeguards.

Pixc.history: Pixc is a human-powered image editing platform that provides professional photo editing services for e-commerce product images, particularly background removal and retouching.
Pixc.summary_abuse: Pixc has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B image editing service for e-commerce merchants with no public-facing hosting or content delivery features that would attract fraudulent actors.

Pixel Motion.history: Pixel Motion is a digital marketing platform specializing in automotive dealerships, providing SEO, website, and lead generation solutions tailored to the automotive retail sector.
Pixel Motion.summary_abuse: Pixel Motion has no notable abuse signal. It is a vertical-specific digital marketing platform for auto dealerships with no public hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Pixelesq.history: Pixelesq is a no-code AI website generation platform that uses artificial intelligence to generate and manage websites from structured content inputs.
Pixelesq.summary_abuse: Pixelesq has a low abuse risk profile. As an AI-powered no-code website builder, it could be used to rapidly spin up low-quality or spam sites, though its niche market and limited scale reduce this risk in practice.

Pixeleze.history: Pixeleze is an affiliate tracking SaaS for ecommerce recording referral sources. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Pixeleze.summary_abuse: Pixeleze is an affiliate tracking tool. Affiliate tracking platforms can be used for fraudulent click farming or cookie stuffing, but no specific documented incidents are known for Pixeleze.

PixelFed.history: PixelFed was created by Daniel Sup in 2018 as a federated, privacy-respecting alternative to Instagram, built on the ActivityPub protocol and the Laravel framework. It is part of the Fediverse ecosystem and gained interest following Twitter/X ownership changes in 2022.
PixelFed.summary_abuse: PixelFed is a federated image-sharing platform. Because anyone can run their own PixelFed instance, moderation quality varies by server operator. There is minimal documented abuse specific to PixelFed itself, though like all Fediverse projects, some instances may have lax moderation policies.

PixelYourSite.history: PixelYourSite is a WordPress plugin developed to consolidate multiple marketing pixel and tag management tasks, including Facebook Pixel, Google Analytics, and advertising platforms. It emerged from the need for non-technical WordPress users to manage complex tracking without custom coding.
PixelYourSite.summary_abuse: PixelYourSite is a WordPress tracking plugin whose primary purpose is to inject ad-tech trackers including Facebook Pixel and Google remarketing tags. Its design inherently embeds heavy tracker density on any WordPress site using it. While it is not a platform for hosting abusive content, it enables surveillance-oriented tracking of website visitors, which is its core function.

Pixenio.history: Pixenio is a responsive website builder SaaS. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Pixenio.summary_abuse: Pixenio is a website builder with no documented abuse signal. As with all hosted builders, it could theoretically host malicious pages.

Pixieset Store.history: Pixieset was founded around 2012 in Canada as a platform for professional photographers to deliver, share, and sell their work online. The Store module was added to enable direct print and digital product sales from client galleries.
Pixieset Store.summary_abuse: Pixieset Store is a photography-focused e-commerce tool with no notable abuse signal. It serves professional photographers selling prints and digital downloads to clients and does not expose infrastructure commonly targeted for fraud.

Pixieset Website.history: Pixieset Website is the portfolio website builder module of the Pixieset platform, founded around 2012 in Canada to serve professional photographers building their online presence.
Pixieset Website.summary_abuse: Pixieset Website is a photography portfolio builder with no notable abuse signal. Its audience is professional photographers and the platform does not provide free custom-domain hosting or anonymous signup in a way that attracts fraudulent use.

PIXIjs.history: PixiJS is a fast 2D rendering engine using WebGL with canvas fallback, first released in 2013 by Mat Groves. It is widely used for interactive animations, HTML5 games, and data visualizations on the web. The project has maintained continuous development and is used by major companies for high-performance 2D graphics.
PIXIjs.summary_abuse: PixiJS has no notable abuse signal. It is a 2D graphics rendering library used by web developers for games and interactive animations, with no consumer-facing surface that fraud operators would target.

Pixlee TurnTo.history: Pixlee was founded in 2012 as a UGC and influencer marketing platform. It merged with TurnTo, a ratings and reviews platform, to form Pixlee TurnTo. In November 2022, Emplifi (the customer engagement platform formed from Socialbakers and Astute Solutions) acquired Pixlee TurnTo, integrating it into its broader customer experience suite.
Pixlee TurnTo.summary_abuse: Pixlee TurnTo is a UGC, ratings, and influencer marketing platform primarily used by brands to aggregate consumer content. It has no documented abuse signal as a platform for fraud or scams, as it targets verified brand customers rather than providing anonymous content hosting.

Pixnet.history: Pixnet is a Taiwanese photo-sharing, blogging, and social networking platform founded in the early 2000s. It became one of the most popular blogging and social media platforms in Taiwan, primarily serving Taiwanese users.
Pixnet.summary_abuse: Pixnet has a low abuse risk for international actors. It is a Taiwanese social networking and blogging platform where users can host content; like most UGC platforms there is some exposure to spam blogs and inappropriate content, but it primarily serves a Taiwanese audience and is not commonly cited in Western security research.

Pixpa.history: Pixpa is an India-based portfolio website builder for photographers, artists, and designers, launched around 2014. It has remained an independent bootstrapped company serving creatives seeking affordable online portfolio solutions.
Pixpa.summary_abuse: Pixpa is a portfolio website builder for photographers and creatives with no notable abuse signal. Its subscription-based model with no free tier reduces the likelihood of anonymous abuse compared to free website builders.

PizzaNetz.history: PizzaNetz is a German ordering and shop system designed specifically for pizzerias, Chinese restaurants, and kebab shops to manage online orders and deliveries.
PizzaNetz.summary_abuse: PizzaNetz has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche restaurant ordering system for specific food types in Germany, with a closed merchant customer base and no public-facing hosting features fraudsters would exploit.

Placed.history: Placed was founded in 2011 in Seattle as a location-based advertising measurement company. It was acquired by Snap Inc. (Snapchat's parent) in 2017 for approximately $135M to enhance Snap's advertising attribution capabilities.
Placed.summary_abuse: Placed is an advertising measurement and location attribution platform owned by Snap Inc. It processes anonymized location data to measure ad campaign effectiveness. As a data analytics backend for advertisers, it has no consumer-facing surface targeted by fraud operators, though its location data collection raises privacy considerations.

Placester.history: Placester was founded in 2011 in Boston, Massachusetts, as a real estate digital marketing platform targeting agents and brokerages. It has raised venture capital funding and serves a large number of real estate professionals across the United States.
Placester.summary_abuse: Placester is a real estate marketing platform with no notable abuse signal. Its verified real estate agent customer base and subscription model make it an unlikely target for fraudulent hosting or scam operations.

Plaid.history: Plaid was founded in 2013 by Zach Perret and William Hockey in San Francisco as a financial data API platform enabling apps to connect to users' bank accounts. It raised $425M at a $13.4B valuation in 2021, but Visa's proposed $5.3B acquisition was blocked by the US DOJ in January 2021 on antitrust grounds. Plaid has since grown independently, reaching an $8B valuation in a 2026 employee tender offer.
Plaid.summary_abuse: Plaid is a financial data infrastructure platform that mediates bank account connections for fintech apps. It holds sensitive financial credentials and has been the subject of privacy litigation: in 2022 it settled a class-action lawsuit for $58M over allegations it collected more financial data than disclosed to users. Plaid publishes a security portal, maintains SOC 2 compliance, and has a bug bounty program. Direct fraud abuse of the platform is uncommon; risk lies in credential handling and data scope concerns.

Plait.history: Plait is a productivity and collaboration SaaS platform designed for healthcare practices to support team coordination and patient engagement.
Plait.summary_abuse: Plait has no notable abuse signal. It is a practice management tool for healthcare providers with no public-facing content hosting or anonymous signup features that would attract fraudulent use.

Planet.history: Planet is an open-source feed aggregator that was widely used in the early 2000s to create planet sites aggregating RSS/Atom blog feeds from communities of developers. The original Python implementation became a classic blogging-era tool.
Planet.summary_abuse: Planet has no notable abuse signal. It is a feed aggregation tool that reads and combines RSS/Atom feeds, with no consumer-facing hosting surface or user-generated content features that fraudsters would exploit.

Planfy.history: Planfy is a UK-based appointment booking SaaS for small businesses, founded around 2016. It provides scheduling and customer management tools for service-oriented businesses.
Planfy.summary_abuse: Planfy is a small appointment booking platform with no documented abuse signal. Its verified business customer base does not present characteristics attractive to fraud operators.

Planio.history: Planio was founded in 2009 in Berlin, Germany, as a hosted project management and issue tracking platform built on the open-source Redmine framework. It has remained an independent bootstrapped company focused on the European market.
Planio.summary_abuse: Planio is a project management and issue tracking SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It serves teams in a B2B subscription model and does not host public content or provide anonymous signup.

Planne.history: Planne is a ticketing and booking reservation system designed for event organizers and venues to manage online reservations and ticket sales.
Planne.summary_abuse: Planne has no notable abuse signal. It is a ticketing reservation system for legitimate events and venues, with no notable exposure to fraud or phishing abuse.

Planning Pod.history: Planning Pod is a US-based all-in-one event management software platform serving event planners and venue managers. It was founded around 2011 and has remained an independent company focused on the event planning vertical.
Planning Pod.summary_abuse: Planning Pod is an event management SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It operates in a professional B2B context serving event planners and venues, and its subscription model does not attract fraudulent hosting.

Plannit.history: Plannit is a task management and home services network CRM. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Plannit.summary_abuse: Plannit is a CRM for home services with no documented abuse signal.

Planoplan.history: Planoplan is a 3D interior design and floor plan tool that enables architects, designers, and real estate professionals to create room layouts, visualizations, and virtual tours directly in a browser.
Planoplan.summary_abuse: Planoplan has no notable abuse signal. It is a 3D design tool for interior layout visualization used by architects and real estate professionals, with no consumer hosting surface relevant to fraud.

Planway.history: Planway is an online booking and appointment scheduling system allowing businesses to manage services, schedules, and customer bookings across various industries.
Planway.summary_abuse: Planway has no notable abuse signal. It is an appointment scheduling tool for service businesses, with no consumer-facing hosting capabilities that fraudsters would typically exploit.

Planyo.history: Planyo is a flexible online reservation system founded in the Netherlands around 2006, designed to handle bookings for diverse services including accommodations, equipment rentals, tours, and classes.
Planyo.summary_abuse: Planyo has no notable abuse signal. It is an online reservation management system for service businesses, with no consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous signup features that fraudsters typically target.

Planzer.history: Planzer is a Swiss transport and logistics company offering warehousing and delivery services primarily in Switzerland and neighboring European countries. It is a traditional logistics business with a digital portal for managing shipments.
Planzer.summary_abuse: Planzer has no notable abuse signal. It is a Swiss logistics and transport company offering business shipping services. Its customer portal is for operational logistics management and has no consumer-facing features that fraudsters would target.

Plasmic.history: Plasmic is a no-code visual headless page builder and content management system founded around 2020, enabling developers and designers to build and manage website content without code. It integrates with existing codebases and frameworks.
Plasmic.summary_abuse: Plasmic has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer-focused visual CMS that integrates with existing codebases, not a public hosting platform; fraudsters would find little utility in targeting it.

Plate.history: Plate is a multisite CMS designed for publishers and media companies to manage and distribute digital content across multiple websites from a single platform.
Plate.summary_abuse: Plate has no notable abuse signal. It is a multisite CMS for enterprise publishers and media organizations with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Platform.sh.history: Platform.sh was founded around 2010 and is headquartered in Paris and San Francisco; it raised $140M in Series D funding in 2022 and later rebranded its product offering as Upsun while continuing to operate the Platform.sh PaaS.
Platform.sh.summary_abuse: Platform.sh is a developer-focused PaaS with no notable abuse signal beyond typical cloud hosting risks; its primarily enterprise and professional developer audience and subscription pricing reduce abuse risk relative to free-tier consumer hosting platforms.

Platforma LP.history: Platforma LP is a web design platform offering ready-to-use website templates and landing page solutions for various industries, primarily targeting Russian-speaking markets.
Platforma LP.summary_abuse: Platforma LP has no notable abuse signal. It is a template-based landing page builder primarily used in Russian-speaking markets for business website creation, with no major documented abuse activity.

Platformly.history: Platformly is an intelligent marketing automation platform for online businesses, offering email marketing, CRM, and analytics tools with a focus on revenue attribution and customer lifecycle tracking.
Platformly.summary_abuse: Platformly has a low abuse risk typical of email marketing platforms. It provides bulk email sending capabilities which could theoretically be used for spam, but as a paid subscription service with a legitimate customer base, mass unsolicited email abuse is limited.

PlatformOS.history: PlatformOS is a Platform-as-a-Service CMS solution offering a developer-focused infrastructure for building web applications, ecommerce sites, and multi-tenant platforms.
PlatformOS.summary_abuse: PlatformOS has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer PaaS for building custom web applications, with no consumer-facing anonymous hosting features that bad actors typically exploit.

PlatinMarket.history: PlatinMarket is a Turkish SaaS e-commerce platform founded in 2004; it provides virtual storefronts with payment management, SEO, and CRM features primarily for the Turkish market.
PlatinMarket.summary_abuse: PlatinMarket is a small Turkish e-commerce SaaS with no notable abuse signal; it serves merchant customers in Turkey and has no characteristics that particularly attract fraudulent use.

Plattar.history: Plattar is an Australian 3D and augmented reality platform founded around 2016, enabling retailers and brands to create immersive product experiences with 3D visualization, AR try-on, and product configuration features.
Plattar.summary_abuse: Plattar has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise AR platform for product visualization, sold to retailers and brands. Its B2B focus and AR technology stack offer no meaningful surface for fraudulent misuse.

Platter.history: Platter is a Shopify app platform that provides integrated themes and apps to streamline store customization and functionality for Shopify merchants.
Platter.summary_abuse: Platter has no notable abuse signal. It is a Shopify theme and app platform for merchants, not a public-facing content hosting service, with no significant fraud exposure.

Plausible.history: Plausible Analytics was founded in 2018 by Uku Taht and Marko Saric as an open-source, privacy-first alternative to Google Analytics; it is headquartered in Estonia, licensed under AGPL-3.0, and has remained bootstrapped and independently operated.
Plausible.summary_abuse: Plausible Analytics is a privacy-first web analytics tool with no notable abuse signal; it collects no personal data, places no cookies, and has no consumer-facing surface that would attract phishing or fraud.

Play.history: Play Framework was originally created by Guillaume Bort in 2007 and became one of the first non-blocking, reactive web frameworks for the JVM. It supports both Scala and Java. Lightbend (formerly Typesafe) became its primary steward after acquiring the Scala ecosystem, though Play is now maintained largely by the open-source community.
Play.summary_abuse: Play Framework has no notable abuse signal. It is a server-side web framework for Java and Scala developers, with no consumer-facing surface or shared-hosting capability that fraudsters would exploit.

Plaza.history: Plaza is an e-commerce platform focused on live video commerce, enabling brands and retailers to engage customers through interactive live shopping experiences.
Plaza.summary_abuse: Plaza has no notable abuse signal. It is a live video commerce platform for brands and retailers, with no consumer-facing anonymous hosting that fraudsters would typically exploit.

Plenigo.history: Plenigo is a German subscription management platform founded around 2012, designed specifically for media companies and publishers to manage digital subscriptions, paywalls, and reader financing.
Plenigo.summary_abuse: Plenigo has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B subscription management platform serving European media publishers, with no consumer-facing features that fraudsters would target.

plentymarkets.history: plentymarkets was founded in 2006 by Jan Griesel in Kassel, Germany as an all-in-one ecommerce ERP platform. The company rebranded to PlentyONE and received strategic investment from PSG Equity to support international growth.
plentymarkets.summary_abuse: plentymarkets is a legitimate ecommerce ERP platform used by SMB merchants. It has no notable abuse signal beyond what is typical for any ecommerce platform.

PlentyONE.history: PlentyONE (formerly plentymarkets) is a German e-commerce platform founded around 2012 offering cloud ERP, multichannel integration, and omnichannel capabilities for mid-market to enterprise retailers.
PlentyONE.summary_abuse: PlentyONE has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise e-commerce ERP platform serving legitimate multi-channel retailers in Europe, with no consumer-facing features that fraudsters would exploit.

plentyShop LTS.history: plentyShop LTS is the official open-source storefront template plugin developed by plentymarkets (PlentyONE) for their ecommerce platform. It is built with Vue.js and serves as the default frontend for plentymarkets stores.
plentyShop LTS.summary_abuse: plentyShop LTS has no notable abuse signal; it is an ecommerce storefront template plugin tied to the plentymarkets platform.

Plerdy.history: Plerdy was founded in 2017 in Ukraine as a conversion rate optimization and UX analytics platform, offering heatmaps, session recordings, funnel analysis, and SEO tools for websites and e-commerce stores.
Plerdy.summary_abuse: Plerdy has no notable abuse signal. It is a website analytics and CRO tool used by marketers and developers. Its session recording and heatmap features collect behavioral data but this is standard in the analytics category and not specifically associated with fraud or phishing.

Pleroma.history: Pleroma was created in 2016-2017 by a pseudonymous developer known as 'Lain' as a lightweight, self-hostable ActivityPub/federated social networking server; it became part of the Fediverse ecosystem alongside Mastodon.
Pleroma.summary_abuse: Pleroma instances have been used by some communities that host content not permitted on mainstream platforms, including instances associated with far-right and hate speech content; as a self-hosted fediverse server with no central moderation, abuse enforcement is entirely at the discretion of individual instance administrators.

Plesk.history: Plesk was founded in 2001 as a web hosting control panel; it was acquired by SWSoft (later Parallels) in 2003, and in 2017 was acquired by Oakley Capital and became part of WebPros, a hosting automation platform group.
Plesk.summary_abuse: Plesk is a widely used server management panel; while individual servers running Plesk may host abusive content that is the responsibility of the server operator, Plesk itself is a legitimate hosting tool with no notable pattern of being preferred by bad actors over alternatives.

Plezi.history: Plezi was founded in 2015 by Charles Dolisy and Renaud de Lacotte in Paris, France, as a B2B marketing automation platform. It is often cited as the first French SaaS marketing automation tool.
Plezi.summary_abuse: Plezi is a B2B marketing automation platform with no notable abuse signal; it is primarily used by French B2B companies for inbound marketing and has no consumer-facing surface that would attract phishing or fraud abuse.

Plices.history: Plices is a marketing and sales automation SaaS. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Plices.summary_abuse: Plices is a marketing automation SaaS with no documented abuse signal. Marketing automation can be used for spam, but no specific incidents are known.

Pliek.history: Pliek is a live sales notifications product sold as a one-time purchase. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Pliek.summary_abuse: Pliek is a fake/live social proof notifications tool. Social proof notification tools are frequently used to display fabricated purchase notifications to manipulate visitors, a documented dark pattern. No specific documented incidents, but the category has documented misuse.

Pligg.history: Pligg was created in 2005 as an open-source PHP CMS for social bookmarking, modeled on Digg and based on the Menéame source code; it was later forked as Kliqqi and is now largely abandoned with minimal maintenance.
Pligg.summary_abuse: Pligg-based sites have historically been popular targets for SEO spam and automated comment/link spam due to the open nature of the platform and its lack of active maintenance; abandoned instances are frequently exploited for backlink injection and spam submission.

Plivo.history: Plivo was founded in 2011 by Venky Bala and Michael Ricordeau, initially as a Y Combinator-backed startup. It became a major cloud communications API platform offering voice and SMS services globally, competing with Twilio in the CPaaS market.
Plivo.summary_abuse: Plivo faces moderate abuse risk typical of cloud communications platforms. SMS and voice APIs are attractive to spammers, scammers running OTP bypass attacks, and bulk message senders. Plivo publishes an AUP and has fraud monitoring, but SMS abuse remains an ongoing challenge across the CPaaS category.

Plone.history: Plone was first released in 2001, built on top of Zope by Alexander Limi and Alan Runyan. It became one of the most security-conscious open-source CMS platforms, widely used by governments, universities, and NGOs. The Plone Foundation was established in 2004 to steward the project.
Plone.summary_abuse: Plone has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted CMS installed by administrators on private infrastructure, with no shared-hosting or anonymous user-generated content features that fraudsters would target.

Plotch.history: Plotch is an Indian ecommerce SaaS leveraging ONDC and Web3 technology. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Plotch.summary_abuse: Plotch is an Indian ecommerce SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Plotly.history: Plotly was founded in 2012 in Montreal, Canada by Jack Parmer, Matthew Sundquist, Alex Johnson, and Chris Parmer; it produces open-source data visualization libraries (plotly.js, Dash) and a commercial hosted analytics platform.
Plotly.summary_abuse: Plotly is a data visualization library with no notable abuse signal; it is a backend/frontend rendering library used by data scientists with no consumer-facing surface attractive to fraudsters.

Plug&Pay.history: Plug&Pay is a Dutch e-commerce payment and checkout platform offering one-click checkout, order bumps, and subscription billing features primarily for digital products and online courses.
Plug&Pay.summary_abuse: Plug&Pay has a low abuse risk typical of checkout payment tools. It processes payments for digital product sales and could be used by fraudulent merchants, but as a regulated European payment service it maintains standard AML and merchant verification requirements.

Pluglin.history: Pluglin is a website translation SaaS supporting multilingual websites. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Pluglin.summary_abuse: Pluglin is a website translation SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Plugo.history: Plugo is a small ecommerce SaaS platform that enables businesses to sell online. No notable ownership changes or significant industry events are recorded.
Plugo.summary_abuse: Plugo is a niche ecommerce platform with no documented abuse history. It provides merchant storefronts but lacks the scale or anonymity features that would make it a preferred tool for fraudulent activity. The free tier is limited and KYC requirements during merchant onboarding reduce casual abuse risk.

Plum Popup.history: Plum Popup is a small SaaS tool for building customizable website popups to boost conversions. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Plum Popup.summary_abuse: Plum Popup is a niche popup widget tool with no documented abuse history. Popup and overlay tools can be used for dark patterns or deceptive UX, but Plum Popup's small scale and paid-only model limit casual abuse risk significantly.

Plupload.history: Plupload was developed by Moxiecode Systems AB as an open-source JavaScript file upload library; it was widely used in the era before native HTML5 upload support became ubiquitous and has since become less actively maintained.
Plupload.summary_abuse: Plupload is a JavaScript file upload library with no notable abuse signal; it is a developer utility with no consumer-facing surface.

Pluralo.history: Pluralo is a small online appointment booking and scheduling SaaS platform. No notable ownership changes or significant industry events are recorded.
Pluralo.summary_abuse: Pluralo is a niche appointment scheduling platform with no documented abuse history. Booking systems pose minimal fraud risk as they facilitate real-world service scheduling rather than hosting arbitrary content.

PlusProComponents.history: PlusProComponents is a front-end component library providing page-level UI components to streamline web development and enhance website design for developers.
PlusProComponents.summary_abuse: PlusProComponents has no notable abuse signal. It is a UI component library for web development with no user-facing hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

PlusThis.history: PlusThis is a marketing automation campaign toolkit that integrates with platforms like ActiveCampaign, Infusionsoft, and Ontraport to provide additional marketing automation capabilities and third-party integrations.
PlusThis.summary_abuse: PlusThis has no notable abuse signal. It is a marketing automation add-on that extends existing CRM platforms, with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would target.

Plutio.history: Plutio is an all-in-one business management platform for freelancers and small agencies, offering project management, time tracking, invoicing, proposals, and client portal features.
Plutio.summary_abuse: Plutio has no notable abuse signal. It is a business management tool for freelancers and small agencies with no public-facing content hosting or anonymous signup features that fraudsters would target.

Plyr.history: Plyr was created by Sam Potts as an open-source MIT-licensed HTML5 media player for video, audio, YouTube, and Vimeo; it has become popular as a lightweight, accessible alternative to native browser controls.
Plyr.summary_abuse: Plyr is an open-source media player library with no notable abuse signal; it is a frontend JavaScript library with no consumer-facing attack surface.

Plytix.history: Plytix is a Danish product information management (PIM) platform founded around 2015, offering AI-powered product data management tools for e-commerce brands to organize and distribute product content across channels.
Plytix.summary_abuse: Plytix has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B PIM platform for managing product catalog data, with no consumer-facing hosting or file-sharing capabilities that fraudsters would exploit.

Po.st.history: Po.st was launched in November 2011 by ad network RadiumOne as a social sharing widget for publishers. It combines social share buttons with analytics and ad revenue generation tied to sharing behavior. RadiumOne used Po.st data to build behavioral targeting profiles for its advertising network.
Po.st.summary_abuse: Po.st carries elevated privacy risk as it is a social sharing widget operated by RadiumOne, an advertising network that used sharing behavior data to build consumer tracking profiles for programmatic advertising. The widget embeds trackers and data collection on any site that deploys it, raising concerns about undisclosed data harvesting. No direct phishing abuse, but its ad-tech nature puts it at the higher end of privacy concern.

Pobo.history: Pobo is a drag-and-drop page builder SaaS aimed at ecommerce merchants for customizing web pages. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Pobo.summary_abuse: Pobo is a niche page builder tool for ecommerce with no documented abuse history. Page builders can theoretically host fake storefronts, but Pobo's small scale and ecommerce merchant focus limit overall abuse risk.

Pobuca.history: Pobuca is a Greek cloud CRM platform specializing in customer loyalty programs and engagement initiatives, targeting SMBs and enterprises seeking to manage customer relationships.
Pobuca.summary_abuse: Pobuca has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B CRM and loyalty platform with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Pocket.history: Pocket was founded in 2007 by Nate Weiner as 'Read It Later' and rebranded to Pocket in 2012; Mozilla acquired it in 2017 for approximately $82M, making it Mozilla's first acquisition.
Pocket.summary_abuse: Pocket is a read-later and content saving service owned by Mozilla with no notable abuse signal; its bookmarking functionality has no significant fraud or phishing surface.

Pocus.history: Pocus is a product-led sales platform founded around 2021, using AI agents to analyze B2B account behavior and surface actionable sales opportunities for revenue teams.
Pocus.summary_abuse: Pocus has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B sales intelligence tool used by enterprise revenue teams, with no consumer-facing or hosting features that fraudsters would target.

Podia.history: Podia was founded in 2014 as Coach and rebranded to Podia in 2017. It is a platform for creators to sell online courses, memberships, and digital downloads without needing separate tools for hosting, payment, and email marketing.
Podia.summary_abuse: Podia has a low abuse risk profile. It enables creators to sell digital products and courses, and like most digital product platforms could theoretically host low-quality or scam digital products, but Podia's paid plans and creator verification reduce this risk. No notable documented abuse incidents.

Podigee.history: Podigee is a German independent podcast hosting and analytics platform founded around 2013, offering distribution, analytics, and monetization services to podcast publishers across Europe.
Podigee.summary_abuse: Podigee has no notable abuse signal. It is a podcast hosting service for legitimate publishers. While podcasting platforms can occasionally host scam or spam audio content, Podigee's European market focus and paid model limit significant abuse exposure.

Podio.history: Podio was founded in 2009 in Copenhagen by Tommy Ahlers, Jon Froda, and others. It became a popular customizable collaboration and project management platform. In 2012 Citrix acquired Podio to add to its enterprise collaboration suite. Citrix itself was later acquired by Vista Equity Partners and Elliott Management in 2022.
Podio.summary_abuse: Podio has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise collaboration and project management platform used by businesses for internal workflows. As a paid subscription service under Citrix, it has no public-facing anonymous hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Podium.history: Podium was founded in 2014 in Lehi, Utah by Eric Rea and Dennis Steele. The platform enables local businesses to collect and manage customer reviews, and expanded to messaging, payments, and webchat. Podium has raised over $400M in funding from investors including YC, Sapphire Ventures, and GV.
Podium.summary_abuse: Podium has a low abuse risk. It is primarily a customer communications platform for local businesses, with no direct file hosting or anonymous sign-up features. As with any messaging platform, SMS and review features could theoretically be misused for spam, but Podium serves a vetted business customer base and has AUP enforcement.

Podkite.history: Podkite is a small podcast analytics and chart ranking tracker SaaS launched around 2020. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Podkite.summary_abuse: Podkite is a podcast analytics tool with no documented abuse history and no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target. It tracks podcast chart rankings and has no meaningful abuse signal.

Podpage.history: Podpage is an automated podcast website builder that creates professional podcast sites by pulling in episode data from RSS feeds, requiring no manual setup from the podcaster.
Podpage.summary_abuse: Podpage has no notable abuse signal. It is a podcast website builder that auto-generates sites from RSS feeds. No consumer-facing anonymous hosting or open file upload features that fraudsters would target.

Podsights.history: Podsights was founded in 2018 as a podcast attribution and measurement platform; in February 2022, Spotify acquired Podsights alongside Chartable to strengthen its podcast advertising analytics capabilities.
Podsights.summary_abuse: Podsights is a podcast attribution service now owned by Spotify; as an ad measurement platform it collects listener behavior data for advertisers, raising standard privacy considerations, but has no notable abuse signal.

POINT.history: POINT is a volunteer management platform and app founded around 2017, helping nonprofits find, recruit, and manage volunteers through a mobile-first platform.
POINT.summary_abuse: POINT has no notable abuse signal. It is a volunteer management platform for nonprofits with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraud operators would target.

Pojo.me.history: Pojo.me is a WordPress accessibility overlay plugin designed to help website owners meet WCAG compliance requirements by adding an accessibility toolbar overlay to any WordPress theme.
Pojo.me.summary_abuse: Pojo.me has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted WordPress accessibility plugin with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would target.

Polar Analytics.history: Polar Analytics was founded in 2021 as an intelligent marketing analytics dashboard for direct-to-consumer and Shopify brands. The company raised an $18M Series A in 2024 to expand its AI-powered data stack for e-commerce marketers in the post-cookie era.
Polar Analytics.summary_abuse: Polar Analytics is a B2B analytics platform for e-commerce brands with no notable abuse signal. It requires a paid contract to access, has no free tier with custom domain hosting, and there are no documented cases of abuse in phishing, scam, or fraud contexts.

POLi Payment.history: POLi Payment (originally Centricom) is an Australian/New Zealand online payment service that enables bank-to-bank direct payments for ecommerce without requiring credit cards. It was founded around 2006 and operates primarily in the Australian and New Zealand markets as an alternative payment method.
POLi Payment.summary_abuse: POLi Payment has no notable abuse signal. It is an Australian/New Zealand bank-transfer payment service used by legitimate merchants; unlike credit card processors, it is less commonly targeted for fraud due to its bank-to-bank transaction model.

Poll Everywhere.history: Poll Everywhere was founded in April 2007 in San Francisco, California, as one of the earliest SMS-based audience response systems for live presentations. The platform pioneered mobile polling for educational and corporate settings and has grown into a mainstream audience engagement tool used by enterprises and universities.
Poll Everywhere.summary_abuse: Poll Everywhere is an audience response system primarily used in presentations and classrooms. Its free tier accepts anonymous responses and could theoretically be used to collect votes or survey data without consent, but this represents a trivially small risk relative to its legitimate use. No documented phishing, scam, or fraud abuse has been attributed to the platform.

Poll Maker.history: Poll Maker is a SaaS platform for creating and managing online polls, serving small businesses and educators. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Poll Maker.summary_abuse: Poll Maker is a niche online polling tool with minimal documented abuse. Like most free-tier form and survey tools, it could theoretically be used to harvest data or create fake engagement, but the vendor category and scale limit systemic risk.

Pollster.history: Instytut Badań Pollster was founded in 2011 in Warsaw, Poland as a market research and public opinion polling company specialising in CAWI (Computer Assisted Web Interviewing) research. The company operates the ReaktorOpinii.pl research panel and ranked 2nd among Polish research firms in 2022 by the number of online interviews conducted.
Pollster.summary_abuse: Pollster is a Polish market research and survey platform with no documented abuse history. It is a B2B research tool used by Polish businesses and the media for opinion polling; it does not offer anonymous content creation features that would attract fraud operators.

Poloda AI.history: Poloda AI is an accessibility compliance solution providing AI-powered tools to help websites meet WCAG and ADA accessibility standards.
Poloda AI.summary_abuse: Poloda AI has no notable abuse signal. It is an accessibility compliance tool for websites with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would target.

Poloriz.history: Poloriz was founded in 2019 in Tel Aviv, Israel by Arik Almog, Amir Barhak, and Nirit Ayal. The company developed an ML-based ecommerce personalisation technology that automatically creates a full-screen, mobile-first cross-selling experience for shoppers. It raised $110K in seed funding and operates with a very small team.
Poloriz.summary_abuse: Poloriz is a very small Israeli ecommerce personalisation startup with no documented abuse history. Its B2B integration model requires merchant setup and does not offer any consumer-facing content hosting features.

Poltio.history: Poltio was founded in 2014 in Istanbul, Turkey, by Ahmet Tosun, Guney Can Gokoglu, and Vildan Ay as a social polling and brand engagement platform. The company later expanded to offer AI-powered product quizzes and guided selling tools for e-commerce brands.
Poltio.summary_abuse: Poltio is a B2B brand engagement and quiz platform with no notable abuse signal. It is primarily used by brands and publishers for customer engagement and there are no documented phishing, scam, or fraud use cases associated with the platform.

Polyfill.history: Polyfill.io was created by Andrew Betts at the Financial Times as an automatic JavaScript polyfill CDN service. Ownership later transferred to community maintainers. In February 2024, the domain and GitHub repository were sold to Funnull, a Chinese CDN company. By June 2024, the new owners were injecting malicious JavaScript into over 100,000 websites, redirecting users to gambling and malware sites. The domain was subsequently taken down by Namecheap. Cloudflare and Fastly created safe alternative mirrors. The service is effectively deprecated; users are advised to avoid cdn.polyfill.io.
Polyfill.summary_abuse: Polyfill.io is one of the most significant supply-chain attack vectors in recent web history. In June 2024, after the domain was sold to Funnull (a Chinese CDN operator), the service began injecting malicious JavaScript into over 100,000 websites that loaded scripts from cdn.polyfill.io. The malicious code targeted mobile users and redirected them to gambling and malware sites. The attack has been linked to North Korean state actors laundering cryptocurrency through the Suncity gambling ecosystem. Security firms, Cloudflare, and Google all issued urgent warnings to remove polyfill.io scripts. The original OSS project on GitHub remains available, but the cdn.polyfill.io delivery endpoint should not be trusted under any circumstances.

Polylang.history: Polylang was created by Frederic Demarle and first released as a WordPress multilingual plugin in 2011. Since 2016, development has been managed by WP Syntex, a company co-founded by Demarle, which also offers a Polylang Pro paid version.
Polylang.summary_abuse: Polylang is a WordPress plugin for creating multilingual sites with no notable abuse signal; it is a backend plugin that does not add consumer-facing content hosting capabilities exploitable for phishing or fraud.

Polymer.history: Polymer is a JavaScript library for building web components, developed by Google starting in 2013. Google released v1.0 in 2015, with major updates through v3.0 in 2018. In 2018, the Polymer team placed all Polymer elements into maintenance mode and began promoting LitElement as a lighter successor; in 2021, LitElement was superseded by Lit as Google's canonical web components library. Polymer itself is now deprecated in favour of Lit.
Polymer.summary_abuse: Polymer is a Google-developed OSS web components library with no notable abuse signal. As a frontend JavaScript library, it has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Popcorn CRM.history: Popcorn CRM is a small visual CRM platform offering essential customer relationship management tools, targeting small businesses and startups. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Popcorn CRM.summary_abuse: Popcorn CRM is a niche CRM tool with no documented abuse history. CRM platforms generally have no meaningful external abuse surface as they are used internally by businesses to manage customer contacts.

Poper.history: Poper is an AI-powered onsite engagement platform offering popups, banners, and personalization for website visitor conversion. Founded around 2021; no notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Poper.summary_abuse: Poper is a niche AI-driven popup and engagement tool with no documented abuse history. Engagement and popup platforms can enable dark patterns but Poper's commercial focus and paid tiers limit systematic misuse.

Popify.history: Popify is a sales push notification SaaS that shows real-time social proof alerts on websites. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Popify.summary_abuse: Popify is a social proof notification tool with no documented abuse history. Such tools could display fabricated or misleading purchase notifications, but the vendor category and subscription model limit systemic abuse potential.

Popmenu.history: Popmenu was founded in 2016 in Atlanta, Georgia, as a restaurant marketing and digital menu platform. The company has grown to serve thousands of restaurants across the US, offering CMS, online menus, ordering, and marketing automation. It has raised significant VC funding and reached $59M ARR by 2024.
Popmenu.summary_abuse: Popmenu is a restaurant marketing and online ordering platform. No notable abuse, phishing, or fraud activity is associated with the service. Its restaurant-focused B2B model and subscription pricing limit exposure to consumer-facing misuse.

Popmix.history: Popmix is a small CRM SaaS platform for managing customer relationships. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Popmix.summary_abuse: Popmix is a niche CRM tool with no documented abuse history. CRM platforms are internally facing business tools with minimal external abuse surface.

Popmotion.history: Popmotion is a low-level JavaScript animation library created by Matt Perry, who also created Framer Motion. The library provides functional animation utilities and has been used as an underlying engine for higher-level animation frameworks. Matt Perry is also the author of Motion One and Framer Motion.
Popmotion.summary_abuse: Popmotion is an OSS JavaScript animation utility library with no notable abuse signal. As a low-level animation package, it has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Popper.history: Popper.js was created by Federico Zerbetto in 2016 as a JavaScript positioning engine for tooltips, dropdowns, and popovers. Popper v2 was released in 2020 with a rewrite. The project later evolved into Floating UI, which is the modern recommended successor to Popper.js, with broader framework support. Popper.js itself entered maintenance mode as development focus shifted to Floating UI.
Popper.summary_abuse: Popper.js is an OSS UI positioning library widely used in web frameworks. It has no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal. It is a foundational frontend utility with no documented involvement in phishing, fraud, or malware.

Poppins.history: Poppins is a website notifications tool offering video and engagement notifications to website visitors. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Poppins.summary_abuse: Poppins is a niche website notification tool with no documented abuse history. Notification widgets could theoretically push misleading content, but the vendor's small scale and commercial model limit systemic risk.

Popsy.history: Popsy is a no-code website builder for creators that uses Notion as a backend, enabling Notion pages to become published websites. It launched around 2020 and has grown organically in the no-code community.
Popsy.summary_abuse: Popsy is a no-code site builder that publishes Notion-backed websites. It offers a free tier with custom domain support, which is a common setup for phishing landing pages. No major incidents are documented, but the free-tier plus custom domain combination warrants modest caution. The vendor is small with no published AUP or abuse reporting channel.

Poptin.history: Poptin was founded in 2017 in Tel Aviv, Israel, as a popup and lead capture SaaS for website owners. The platform offers a free-forever tier and has grown organically without major acquisitions or ownership changes.
Poptin.summary_abuse: Poptin is a popup and lead-capture SaaS with a free tier that allows website operators to deploy overlays on their sites. Popup tools are frequently used to create fake urgency countdowns, misleading opt-in forms, and dark-pattern consent flows, and the free tier with no strong KYC lowers the barrier to misuse. No specific documented phishing or fraud campaign has been attributed to Poptin, but the category is inherently higher risk for dark patterns.

PopularFX.history: PopularFX is a free, customizable responsive WordPress theme available on the WordPress theme directory. It was released by its developer around 2016 with a companion premium plugin for extended features. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
PopularFX.summary_abuse: PopularFX is a WordPress theme with no documented abuse history. As a self-hosted WordPress component it has no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would target directly.

Popup Maker.history: Popup Maker is an open-source WordPress plugin that allows site owners to create and customize popup windows. It has been available since 2014 and is one of the most widely installed popup plugins in the WordPress repository, with millions of active installations. The project is maintained by WP popup community developers.
Popup Maker.summary_abuse: Popup Maker is a self-hosted WordPress plugin with no inherent abuse vector. It runs on user-controlled infrastructure and is most commonly used for legitimate marketing and newsletter sign-ups. As with any popup tool, it can be misused for deceptive consent flows or dark patterns, but abuse is in the hands of the site operator, not the plugin vendor.

Popupular.history: Popupular is a small no-code popup platform that enables embedding of Mailchimp forms, YouTube videos, Calendly calendars, and similar third-party widgets into website popups. No notable ownership changes or significant events are recorded.
Popupular.summary_abuse: Popupular is a niche popup widget tool with no documented abuse history. It embeds third-party tools like Mailchimp and Calendly; the embedded content risk depends on those upstream services rather than Popupular itself.

Popvox.history: POPVOX was founded in 2010 in Redwood City, California as a civic engagement platform to connect citizens with their elected representatives and allow them to express policy positions on legislation. The company raised $2M in funding and also established a related nonprofit entity, POPVOX Foundation, to support broader civic infrastructure and democratic institution research.
Popvox.summary_abuse: POPVOX is a civic technology platform for legislative engagement with no documented abuse history. It handles citizen-to-government communications rather than financial or identity data, and operates in the public interest civic tech space without notable fraud or phishing exposure.

Porch.history: Porch was founded in 2012 in Seattle, Washington, as an online home improvement marketplace connecting homeowners with contractors. The company expanded into a vertical SaaS platform serving moving companies, home services, and insurance, and went public via SPAC in late 2020. In 2019, Porch acquired HireAHelper to expand its moving services capabilities.
Porch.summary_abuse: Porch is a publicly traded vertical SaaS company serving the moving and home improvement industry. There is no notable abuse, phishing, or fraud signal associated with the platform. Its public company status and B2B focus reduce exposure to consumer-facing misuse.

Portal.history: Portal (portal.ir) is an Iranian web hosting and CMS platform providing hosting, domain registration, and website management tools. It operates primarily in the Iranian market and has been active since the mid-2000s. No notable ownership changes are recorded.
Portal.summary_abuse: Portal is an Iranian CMS and hosting platform. Its Iran-based operation places it outside standard Western compliance frameworks (GDPR, SOC 2), and Iranian hosting providers are subject to government oversight under Iranian law. There is no specific documented international abuse, but its jurisdiction creates compliance uncertainty for non-Iranian users.

Posh.history: Posh is a conversational AI platform for financial services companies, providing virtual banking assistants and NLP-powered customer service automation for credit unions and banks.
Posh.summary_abuse: Posh has no notable abuse signal. It is a conversational AI platform deployed by financial institutions for customer service automation, with no consumer-facing anonymous features that fraudsters would exploit.

Poshmark.history: Poshmark was founded in 2011 by Manish Chandra, Tracy Sun, Gautam Golwala, and Chetan Pungaliya in Redwood City, California. It grew into one of the largest social commerce platforms for secondhand fashion in the US. The company went public on Nasdaq in January 2021 and was acquired by South Korean internet company Naver in January 2023 for approximately $1.2 billion.
Poshmark.summary_abuse: Poshmark has a moderate abuse profile typical of C2C marketplaces. Common patterns include counterfeit luxury goods listings, non-delivery fraud where sellers take payment without shipping, account takeovers used to drain wallets, and fake item condition misrepresentation. The platform has buyer and seller protection programs and a dedicated trust and safety team, but as a large open marketplace it sees a meaningful volume of scam listings. The free tier with no upfront seller verification creates some exposure to fraudulent listings.

Posify.history: Posify was founded around 2015 as Asia's first O2O (online-to-offline) retail platform, headquartered in Singapore, providing integrated ecommerce and POS solutions primarily for SMEs in Asia.
Posify.summary_abuse: Posify is a retail management SaaS platform with no notable abuse signal. It is an ecommerce and POS system used by legitimate retail businesses and has no documented history of being exploited for fraud, phishing, or malicious hosting.

Poski.history: Poski.com is a Czech ecommerce and web platform provider based in Ostrava, active for over 20 years. The company offers real estate software (Poski REAL), ecommerce platforms, and CMS solutions for regional SME clients.
Poski.summary_abuse: Poski is a regional Czech ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves legitimate small and medium businesses with online store and CMS tools and has no documented history of exploitation by bad actors.

Post Affiliate Pro.history: Post Affiliate Pro was developed by Quality Unit, a software company founded in Slovakia, and has been on the market since 2004 as an affiliate tracking and management platform. It is offered both as SaaS and as on-premise software.
Post Affiliate Pro.summary_abuse: Post Affiliate Pro is an affiliate marketing tracking platform; affiliate marketing platforms have moderate abuse exposure since they can be used to create tracking links for spam or fake referral networks. The platform is primarily used by legitimate ecommerce businesses and has standard terms prohibiting abusive practices.

Post Dolphin.history: Post Dolphin is a social media scheduling and automation tool developed under the Dolphin Suite brand, offering shoppable posts, auto-posting, and content synchronization across Instagram, Facebook, X, and Pinterest. Limited public information is available about the company's exact founding date or ownership structure.
Post Dolphin.summary_abuse: Post Dolphin is a social media scheduling tool with no documented abuse history. Social media automation tools can be used for spam posting, but Post Dolphin is a niche subscription product with no documented reports of misuse.

Postach.history: Postach is a blogging platform that integrates with Evernote, allowing users to publish notes as blog posts directly from their Evernote notebooks. Activity has been dormant in recent years.
Postach.summary_abuse: Postach has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Evernote-integrated blogging platform with a limited user base, offering no significant public anonymous hosting features that fraudsters would target.

Postano.history: Postano is a social media content aggregation platform that collects, curates, and displays UGC from social networks on digital displays and websites for brands and events.
Postano.summary_abuse: Postano has no notable abuse signal beyond the general risk of any UGC aggregation platform. It is a social content display tool for brands and appears largely dormant today.

Poste Italiane.history: Poste Italiane is the Italian national postal service, with origins dating to 1862 as a government entity. It was incorporated as a company in 1998 and partially privatised through an IPO in 2015. The company has since expanded into financial services, insurance, and digital payment services alongside its postal operations.
Poste Italiane.summary_abuse: Poste Italiane is a major national postal and financial services institution. Like other postal services, it is a frequent target of impersonation in phishing campaigns, where scammers send fake delivery SMS or email notifications. The actual Poste Italiane organization publishes fraud-awareness guidance for consumers. No abuse is attributable to Poste Italiane own systems.

Poster.history: Poster (poster.ooo) is a live chat and visitor segmentation SaaS tool. It is a small, relatively new entrant with limited public history available.
Poster.summary_abuse: Poster is a live chat and segmentation tool with no notable abuse signal. It collects visitor data for segmentation purposes which carries inherent privacy considerations, but there is no documented history of significant exploitation by bad actors.

Posterous.history: Posterous was founded in May 2008 by Sachin Agarwal, Brett Gibson, and Garry Tan as a YCombinator-backed email-to-blog publishing platform. Twitter acquired the team in March 2012. The service was shut down on April 30, 2013.
Posterous.summary_abuse: Posterous was a blogging platform shut down in 2013 and has no current abuse signal; the service is defunct and no longer accessible.

PostgreSQL.history: PostgreSQL originated from the POSTGRES project at UC Berkeley, led by Michael Stonebraker, starting in 1986. The open-source PostgreSQL project was launched in 1996 under the PostgreSQL License (a permissive license similar to BSD). It is governed by the PostgreSQL Global Development Group and has no single corporate owner. It is widely considered the most advanced open-source relational database system in the world.
PostgreSQL.summary_abuse: PostgreSQL has no notable abuse signal. It is a foundational open-source relational database engine with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target. Database engines are backend infrastructure tools and do not appear in phishing, scam, or fraud toolkits.

PostHog.history: PostHog was founded in 2020 by James Hawkins and Tim Glaser as an open-source, self-hostable product analytics platform, distinguished from competitors like Mixpanel by its open-source first approach and self-hosting capabilities. The company joined Y Combinator's W20 batch and raised significant VC funding. PostHog has grown into an all-in-one developer platform covering session replay, feature flags, A/B testing, and a data warehouse.
PostHog.summary_abuse: PostHog is an open-source product analytics platform used by development teams. It is a backend analytics infrastructure tool with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target. There is no notable abuse signal associated with PostHog.

Postie.history: Postie was founded in 2017 by Dave Fink and Jonathan Neddenriep, both former executives of Science Inc., and launched publicly in 2018 as an automated direct mail marketing platform. The company raised $3.5M in seed funding from Bonfire Ventures and Crosscut Ventures.
Postie.summary_abuse: Postie is a direct mail marketing automation platform with no notable abuse signal. It enables programmatic direct mail campaigns using audience data, which raises some privacy considerations, but the platform targets enterprise and DTC marketers and has no documented history of exploitation by spammers or fraudsters.

Postman API Documentation.history: Postman was founded in 2012 by Abhinav Asthana, Ankit Sobti, and Abhijit Kane as an API development and testing tool, originally as a Chrome extension. The company is headquartered in San Francisco. Postman raised over $433 million in funding, reaching unicorn status, and has grown into the industry-standard API platform used by millions of developers worldwide.
Postman API Documentation.summary_abuse: Postman is the leading API development and documentation platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a developer productivity tool used by engineers for API testing and documentation, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

PostNL.history: PostNL traces its origins to the Dutch postal service, which dates back to the 18th century. As TPG Post then TNT, the company rebranded to PostNL in 2011 when TNT split its postal and express divisions. PostNL is publicly traded on Euronext Amsterdam and is the dominant postal and parcel carrier in the Netherlands.
PostNL.summary_abuse: PostNL is a major postal and parcel carrier. Logistics companies like PostNL are commonly impersonated in phishing campaigns using fake delivery SMS or email notifications, a well-documented pattern in the Netherlands. PostNL itself is the victim of impersonation rather than the enabler and maintains consumer fraud-awareness communications. No abuse is directly attributable to PostNL own systems.

Postpay.history: Postpay is a buy-now-pay-later fintech company founded around 2019 in the UAE, offering instalment payment solutions for e-commerce merchants in the Middle East. The company focuses on the MENA region market.
Postpay.summary_abuse: Postpay is a BNPL payment solution operating in the MENA region. Buy-now-pay-later services carry elevated fraud risk because they can be exploited with stolen identities or synthetic identities to make purchases without genuine intent to repay. As a payment service, Postpay handles PII and financial data. No specific large-scale fraud incidents tied to Postpay are publicly documented, but the BNPL category generally faces identity fraud and friendly fraud challenges.

Postscript.history: Postscript was founded in 2018 as an SMS and MMS marketing platform purpose-built for Shopify merchants. The company raised over $100 million in VC funding and operates primarily in the US e-commerce market. Postscript is focused on permission-based SMS marketing for DTC brands.
Postscript.summary_abuse: Postscript is an SMS marketing platform for Shopify stores. SMS marketing platforms carry inherent abuse risk because bulk SMS can be used for spam, phishing texts, or unwanted promotional messages. Postscript requires opt-in consent from subscribers and complies with TCPA regulations, which reduces but does not eliminate the spam risk. No specific large-scale fraud or scam campaigns are documented for Postscript specifically, but the category is on watchlists for unauthorized commercial SMS.

Potions.history: Potions is a French e-commerce personalization platform that customizes the shopping experience for site visitors without using cookies, offering cookieless product recommendations and user experience personalization.
Potions.summary_abuse: Potions has no notable abuse signal. It is a personalization engine for e-commerce sites with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

PouchDB.history: PouchDB is an open-source JavaScript database that runs in the browser and synchronizes with CouchDB-compatible servers. It was created by Mikeal Rogers and first released in 2012. The project is used for offline-first web applications and is hosted on GitHub under the Apache 2.0 license. The project is community-maintained with no single corporate sponsor.
PouchDB.summary_abuse: PouchDB is an open-source client-side JavaScript database with no notable abuse signal. As a backend database library for web applications, it has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Powa.history: Powa (powa.com) is a WordPress site speed plugin that operates its own CDN. This is distinct from the collapsed Powa Technologies unicorn (UK, founded 2007, bankrupt 2016) which was an ecommerce/mobile commerce company. The current powa.com CDN product appears to be a separate, unrelated French venture.
Powa.summary_abuse: Powa is a CDN/site speed service for WordPress with no notable abuse signal. CDN services can theoretically be used to serve malicious content, but Powa is a niche product with no documented exploitation for fraud or malware hosting.

Power CRM.history: Power CRM is a specialized CRM platform tailored for vehicle protection associations, offering operational management and customer relationship tools for that specific industry vertical.
Power CRM.summary_abuse: Power CRM has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche CRM for vehicle protection associations with a closed industry client base and no consumer-facing features that fraudsters would target.

Powerboutique.history: Powerboutique is a French e-commerce platform designed for online retail, offering a hosted storefront solution for businesses to sell products online.
Powerboutique.summary_abuse: Powerboutique has no notable abuse signal. It is a French e-commerce hosting platform for legitimate merchants with no notable documented fraudulent use patterns.

Powerfolio.history: Powerfolio is a portfolio plugin for Elementor page builder, providing customizable portfolio layouts and styling options for WordPress-based designer and creative agency websites.
Powerfolio.summary_abuse: Powerfolio has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted WordPress/Elementor portfolio plugin used by designers and agencies, with no consumer-facing hosting surface fraudsters would exploit.

Powerful Form.history: Powerful Form is a Shopify app that enables merchants to create custom forms for customer data collection, contact requests, and survey interactions within Shopify stores.
Powerful Form.summary_abuse: Powerful Form has no notable abuse signal. It is a Shopify form builder for merchants, not a public-facing service with open hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Powergap.history: POWERGAP AG is a German e-commerce shop system with integrated inventory management and POS functionality, developed since 2001 and based in Waldbröl, North Rhine-Westphalia. It serves approximately 200 managed shops primarily in Germany and the EU. No notable ownership changes since founding.
