Powergap.summary_abuse: Powergap has no notable abuse signal; it is a niche German e-commerce platform for mid-market retailers with managed hosting and no anonymous signup that would attract fraud operators.

PowerPay.history: PowerPay is a US-based consumer financing and loan platform offering lower-than-average interest rates for home improvement and other services, enabling contractors and merchants to offer financing options to customers.
PowerPay.summary_abuse: PowerPay has a low-moderate abuse risk typical of consumer financing platforms. Merchant fraud and identity theft for loan applications are known risks in the consumer lending space. As a regulated lender PowerPay is subject to KYC/AML requirements, but financing platforms can be targeted by fraudulent merchants or synthetic identity fraud.

PowerReviews.history: PowerReviews was founded in 2005 as a provider of ratings and reviews software for retailers and brands. The platform helps companies collect and display user-generated review content. It went through private equity ownership and has remained an independent player in the UGC reviews market.
PowerReviews.summary_abuse: PowerReviews has a low abuse risk. It is a UGC ratings and reviews platform for e-commerce. Fake reviews are an industry-wide concern, but the primary risk lies with merchants manipulating review scores rather than external fraud actors exploiting the platform for phishing or malware.

PowerSchool.history: PowerSchool was created in 1997 by Greg Porter as a student information system for K-12 schools. Apple acquired it in 2001 for $62 million, then sold it to Pearson in 2006. Vista Equity Partners acquired it from Pearson in 2015 and significantly expanded it through 18 acquisitions. PowerSchool went public in 2021, and in October 2024, Bain Capital took it private in a $5.6 billion deal.
PowerSchool.summary_abuse: PowerSchool has no notable abuse signal as a K-12 student information system. It handles sensitive student data (grades, attendance, personal information) and has faced scrutiny over data privacy for minors, but the platform is not used in external fraud or phishing contexts. A 2024 data breach exposed student records, highlighting the sensitivity of data it holds.

POWR.history: POWR.io was founded around 2014 as a cloud-based widget and plugin platform allowing website owners to add interactive elements like forms, galleries, and social feeds to any website. It serves millions of websites across various CMS platforms.
POWR.summary_abuse: POWR has a moderate risk profile as a freely embeddable widget platform. Free-tier widgets that collect user data (forms, email capture) can be embedded on phishing pages to harvest credentials or personal information. The platform's ease of use and free tier lower the barrier for misuse, though no major documented abuse campaigns are specifically linked to POWR.

Powster.history: Powster was founded in 2009 as a London-based digital creative studio specialising in entertainment marketing. It serves over 150 movie distributors including all six major Hollywood studios, and has expanded into analytics and interactive experiences for music and film. No notable ownership changes since founding.
Powster.summary_abuse: Powster has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B creative studio for entertainment brands with no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract fraud or phishing activity.

Powtoon.history: Powtoon was founded in January 2012 by Ilya Spitalnik, Daniel Zaturansky, and Sven Hoffmann, with roots in Israel before establishing UK headquarters. Its name is a portmanteau of PowerPoint and cartoon. It raised initial funding of $600,000 from Startup Minds in December 2012 and has grown into a mainstream animated video and presentation platform.
Powtoon.summary_abuse: Powtoon has a low abuse profile. Its free tier could theoretically be used to create phishing-style animated videos or fake training content, but there is no documented pattern of systematic abuse. The platform targets business users and educators rather than anonymous consumers, limiting its attractiveness for fraud operations.

Practice Perfect.history: Practice Perfect is a cloud-based EMR and practice management system for allied health practitioners, offering scheduling, billing, documentation, and patient management tools primarily used by physiotherapists and rehabilitation clinics in Canada.
Practice Perfect.summary_abuse: Practice Perfect has no notable abuse signal. It is a healthcare practice management system used by medical practitioners for patient scheduling and EMR. It handles protected health data in a closed B2B context with no consumer-facing features fraudsters would target.

Practo.history: Practo was founded in 2008 by Shashank ND and Abhinav Lal in India. It became one of India's leading healthcare technology platforms, enabling patients to search for doctors, book appointments, and access health records. The company raised significant VC funding and expanded to Southeast Asia and Brazil, though international expansion was later scaled back.
Practo.summary_abuse: Practo has a low abuse risk overall. It is a healthcare appointment platform primarily used in India. Fake doctor profile listings and fraudulent clinic accounts are occasionally reported concerns on healthcare directories, but the platform is a legitimate and well-established service in its market.

Praedium.history: Praedium is a Brazilian CRM platform offering subscription-based customer relationship management services at low pricing, primarily targeting small and medium businesses in Brazil.
Praedium.summary_abuse: Praedium has no notable abuse signal. It is a Brazilian CRM for B2B sales teams with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Prater Raines.history: Prater Raines Ltd is a micro web development company based in Folkestone, Kent, UK, incorporated in February 2007. It specialises in accessible and sustainable websites and digital systems for businesses and political parties, and operates with approximately 4 employees.
Prater Raines.summary_abuse: Prater Raines has no notable abuse signal; it is a tiny UK web development agency with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

Preact.history: Preact was created by Jason Miller in 2015 as a 3 kB alternative to React with the same modern API. It gained significant adoption due to its minimal footprint and React compatibility layer (preact/compat), and remains actively maintained as an independent open source project.
Preact.summary_abuse: Preact has no notable abuse signal; it is a foundational JavaScript UI library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Prebid.history: Prebid.js was created around 2015 as an open-source header bidding library for digital publishers. It is now governed by Prebid.org, a nonprofit foundation, and has grown into a suite of products (Prebid.js, Prebid Server, Prebid Mobile) that is the dominant open-source header bidding solution used by major publishers worldwide.
Prebid.summary_abuse: Prebid is an open-source ad tech library used by publishers for header bidding. Its ad-serving nature means it is embedded on high-traffic sites and processes user bidding signals; malicious adapters or supply chain compromises could theoretically inject tracking or malicious bids, but such incidents have been rare and the Prebid.org foundation maintains code review. Legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates.

Prediggo.history: Prediggo SA was founded in 2008 in Lausanne, Switzerland, as an AI-driven e-commerce personalisation and recommendation platform based on research from the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology (EPFL). It serves retailers such as Mammut, MediaMarkt, and Intermarche Drive. No notable ownership changes documented.
Prediggo.summary_abuse: Prediggo has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B e-commerce personalisation SaaS with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

Preeco.history: Preeco is a German compliance SaaS platform from Ulm, providing GDPR, NIS2, EU Whistleblower Directive, and ESG compliance management software. It stores all data in ISO 27001-certified German data centres (Hetzner) with no data transfers outside the EU. Over 500 companies use the platform.
Preeco.summary_abuse: Preeco has no notable abuse signal; it is a GDPR and compliance management platform with sensitive regulatory data handled in a fully EU-hosted, ISO 27001 environment, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Preezie.history: Preezie was founded in 2017 in Melbourne, Australia, by Quoc Nguyen, Luke Milkovic, and Michael Tutek, to bring in-store guided selling experiences to e-commerce. It raised $5.5 million in a Series A round in November 2021 led by Touch Ventures (Afterpay-backed). In 2024 it launched an AI shopping assistant built in collaboration with Microsoft.
Preezie.summary_abuse: Preezie has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B retail personalisation SaaS embedding guided selling quizzes on merchant sites with no independent hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

Prefix-Free.history: Prefix-Free was created by Lea Verou in 2011 as a lightweight JavaScript polyfill that automatically adds browser vendor prefixes to CSS. As browsers moved away from vendor prefixes the problem it solved disappeared, and the project is now effectively abandoned but remains available on GitHub.
Prefix-Free.summary_abuse: Prefix-Free has no notable abuse signal; it is an obsolete CSS polyfill library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Preline UI.history: Preline UI is an open-source Tailwind CSS component library created by HTML Stream. It offers 640+ components, dark mode support, Figma design system, and framework guides for React, Vue and others. Launched around 2022 alongside the rise of Tailwind CSS adoption.
Preline UI.summary_abuse: Preline UI has no notable abuse signal; it is a frontend UI component library with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

Premio Chaty.history: Premio Chaty is a WordPress chat plugin developed by Premio, an Israeli WordPress plugin company. It enables website visitors to contact site owners through various messaging channels like WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, and more, via a floating chat widget.
Premio Chaty.summary_abuse: Premio Chaty has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted WordPress chat widget for connecting visitors with site owners via messaging platforms. No consumer-facing hosting or anonymous content features that fraudsters would exploit.

Premion.history: Premion is an OTT/CTV advertising platform launched by TEGNA, a major US TV broadcast company, around 2016. It provides advertisers with targeted ad delivery across premium streaming video content and connected TV devices.
Premion.summary_abuse: Premion has a low abuse risk. As an OTT advertising platform operated by TEGNA, a public media company, it focuses on premium video advertising inventory. The heavy tracker density reflects its ad-tech nature rather than direct fraud or phishing exposure.

Premmerce.history: Premmerce is a Ukrainian e-commerce management platform offering hosted and custom WooCommerce management solutions for online businesses.
Premmerce.summary_abuse: Premmerce has no notable abuse signal. It is a WooCommerce management platform for legitimate merchants with no consumer-facing anonymous hosting features that fraudsters would target.

Prepr.history: Prepr was founded in 2016 in Utrecht, Netherlands by Tim Hanssen and co-developers as a CMS initially targeting media organizations. The company pivoted to a headless CMS with built-in A/B testing and personalization and raised EUR 2.25 million in a pre-Series A round in early 2022 from Newion and other investors.
Prepr.summary_abuse: Prepr has no notable abuse signal; it is a headless CMS serving B2B clients for content management, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

PreProduct.history: PreProduct is a Shopify pre-order management tool that enables merchants to accept orders for out-of-stock or upcoming products, manage demand forecasting, and control pre-order workflows in their Shopify stores.
PreProduct.summary_abuse: PreProduct has no notable abuse signal. It is a Shopify pre-order app used by merchants for demand management. No consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would target.

Press Customizr.history: Customizr is a multipurpose WordPress theme created by the French developer Nicolas Guillaume around 2013. It is distributed on WordPress.org under the GPL license and has a freemium model with a Pro version. No notable ownership changes documented.
Press Customizr.summary_abuse: Press Customizr has no notable abuse signal; it is a WordPress theme with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

Press Hueman.history: Press Hueman (also known as Hueman) is a WordPress theme created by the French company Presscustomizr around 2013. It is one of the most popular free WordPress themes in its niche, with a premium pro version available.
Press Hueman.summary_abuse: Press Hueman is a WordPress theme with no notable abuse signal. It is a presentation-layer tool that does not expose content-hosting, file upload, or user-generated content features.

Pressable.history: Pressable was founded in 2010 as ZippyKid in San Antonio, Texas, offering managed WordPress hosting. It rebranded to Pressable in 2013 and was acquired by Automattic in 2015, becoming part of the same company that owns WordPress.com, WooCommerce, and Jetpack.
Pressable.summary_abuse: Pressable is a managed WordPress hosting platform backed by Automattic. Like most web hosting providers, hosted sites could be used for spam or phishing, but Automattic enforces a published AUP and has responsive abuse handling. No notable abuse incidents specific to Pressable documented.

Pressero.history: Pressero is a web-to-print storefront platform serving print service providers, allowing them to offer customizable print products online. It has operated as an independent SaaS company in the print industry vertical with no notable ownership changes.
Pressero.summary_abuse: Pressero is a B2B web-to-print platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves print service providers and their clients, with no consumer-facing features commonly exploited for phishing or scams.

PressMaximum Customify.history: PressMaximum Customify is a lightweight, flexible, multipurpose WordPress theme developed by PressMaximum, offering high customizability for various website types.
PressMaximum Customify.summary_abuse: PressMaximum Customify has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted WordPress theme with no consumer-facing hosting or content features that fraudsters would exploit.

PrestaShop.history: PrestaShop originated as a student project in 2005 and was formally commercialized when PrestaShop SA was founded in 2007 by Igor Schlumberger and Bruno Levesque in France. It became the leading open-source e-commerce platform in Europe and Latin America, generating over 22 billion euros in online sales in 2024. The company has taken institutional funding to support growth while keeping the core software open-source.
PrestaShop.summary_abuse: PrestaShop has a modest abuse signal as an open-source e-commerce platform. Its self-hosted nature means individual store installations can be set up for fake storefronts or scam shops, and several vulnerabilities have been exploited in the wild (notably SQL injection CVEs in 2022-2023 used to steal card data). The platform itself is not inherently abusive but unpatched deployments are a documented vector for card-skimming attacks.

Presto Player.history: Presto Player is a WordPress video and audio plugin that provides an optimized media player with features like chapters, email capture, and action bars. It was created as a premium WordPress plugin product.
Presto Player.summary_abuse: Presto Player is a WordPress plugin with no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted video player tool with no consumer-facing platform that could be exploited for phishing or spam.

Pretty Damn Quick.history: Pretty Damn Quick (PDQ) is a Shopify checkout optimisation app co-founded by Avi Moskowitz, based in Israel. The company focuses on personalised delivery experience and checkout testing for Shopify merchants, operating as PDQ Logistics Ltd. in the Shopify Partners ecosystem.
Pretty Damn Quick.summary_abuse: Pretty Damn Quick is a Shopify-integrated checkout and delivery optimisation app with no documented abuse history. It is a B2B merchant tool operating within the Shopify ecosystem, with no consumer-facing features that would make it a fraud vector.

Pretty Links.history: Pretty Links is a WordPress plugin for URL shortening, link cloaking, and branded links, launched around 2009. It has grown into one of the most popular WordPress link management plugins with over 300,000 active installs.
Pretty Links.summary_abuse: Pretty Links enables URL shortening and link cloaking within WordPress, which carries moderate abuse potential. Cloaked affiliate links and redirect chains can obscure phishing destinations, though the plugin itself is primarily used legitimately for affiliate marketing. The self-hosted nature limits centralized abuse tracking.

prettyPhoto.history: prettyPhoto is a jQuery-based lightbox clone that adds media popup functionality to web pages. It was popular in the late 2000s and early 2010s but has been largely superseded by more modern alternatives.
prettyPhoto.summary_abuse: prettyPhoto has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side media lightbox plugin.

Price By Country.history: Price By Country is a WooCommerce plugin developed by WooCommerce (Automattic subsidiary) that allows store owners to set geo-specific product prices; it is part of the official WooCommerce extensions marketplace.
Price By Country.summary_abuse: Price By Country is a WooCommerce pricing plugin with no notable abuse signal. It is a backend configuration tool with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

PriceSpider.history: PriceSpider was founded in 2004 in Irvine, California, originally as a consumer shopping engine. In 2012 it pivoted to a B2B SaaS model serving consumer brand manufacturers with retail analytics and price tracking. In 2019 it celebrated its 15th anniversary serving over 1,300 brands.
PriceSpider.summary_abuse: PriceSpider is a retail analytics and price-tracking platform for consumer brands with no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B analytics product with no consumer-facing features that would attract phishing or fraud use.

Priice.history: Priice has operated since approximately 2012 as a price tracking and product locator widget service for consumer brands and distributors, offering a Where To Buy solution, price watcher, and store locator embed. Very limited public information is available about the company's ownership or location.
Priice.summary_abuse: Priice is a price-tracking widget service with no documented abuse signal. It embeds on brand websites to display retailer pricing data and does not host consumer-generated content.

Prima Software.history: Prima Software was founded in 1998 in the United Kingdom and specialises in cloud-based ecommerce and ERP software for the office supplies and business products industry. The company launched its cloud platform in 2014 and has since grown to serve over 2,500 dealers across Europe and North America. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are recorded.
Prima Software.summary_abuse: Prima Software is a niche B2B ecommerce platform targeting the office supplies trade and has no notable abuse signal. Its closed commercial deployment model, industry-specific focus, and SMB customer base do not create meaningful attack surfaces exploited by fraudsters.

Prime AI.history: Prime AI is an AI-powered body shape measurement and garment fit technology company, helping fashion retailers match customers to their best-fit sizes using computer vision and machine learning. It operates as a B2B SaaS for ecommerce and in-store retail.
Prime AI.summary_abuse: Prime AI is a B2B AI fit-technology platform with no notable abuse signal. It processes body measurement data which raises privacy considerations, but the technology is embedded in merchant flows and not consumer-facing in a way that facilitates fraud.

PrimeGate.history: PrimeGate is a Russian end-to-end analytics platform offering call tracking, CRM integration, and marketing analytics for businesses primarily in Russian-speaking markets.
PrimeGate.summary_abuse: PrimeGate is a Russian analytics and call-tracking platform with limited international exposure. Its Russian origin warrants caution regarding data handling practices under Russian data laws, though no specific abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Primeleads.history: Primeleads is a German-based website visitor identification and personalization SaaS. It identifies anonymous website visitors and enables personalized experiences, a category that intersects with data privacy regulations. No notable ownership or acquisition events found.
Primeleads.summary_abuse: Primeleads identifies anonymous website visitors using tracking technology, which raises privacy concerns but is not itself a tool exploited by bad actors. The visitor-identification category has faced regulatory scrutiny under GDPR, and such tools may be used without proper consent by some customers, but the vendor itself has no documented history of facilitating fraud or malware.

PrimeNG.history: PrimeNG is an Angular UI component library developed by PrimeTek, the same company behind PrimeFaces, PrimeReact, and PrimeVue. PrimeTek was founded in Turkey and has built a large open-source UI ecosystem used by millions of developers worldwide.
PrimeNG.summary_abuse: PrimeNG has no notable abuse signal; it is a foundational Angular UI component library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

PrimeReact.history: PrimeReact is a React UI component library developed by PrimeTek as part of the Prime UI ecosystem that also includes PrimeNG (Angular) and PrimeVue (Vue). PrimeTek was founded in Turkey and provides MIT-licensed components used widely in enterprise React applications.
PrimeReact.summary_abuse: PrimeReact has no notable abuse signal; it is a foundational React UI component library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

PrimeVue.history: PrimeVue is a Vue.js UI component library developed by PrimeTek as part of the Prime UI ecosystem. It is the newest addition to the Prime family, offering design-agnostic, accessible components for Vue 3, distributed under the MIT license.
PrimeVue.summary_abuse: PrimeVue has no notable abuse signal; it is a Vue.js UI component library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Primis.history: Primis (formerly known as Playbuzz's video discovery unit) is an Israeli video discovery and monetization platform for publishers. It provides video content recommendations and ad-supported video players embedded on publisher sites.
Primis.summary_abuse: Primis is a video discovery advertising network that embeds on publisher websites, carrying significant tracker density for ad targeting. Video ad networks of this type are sometimes abused for malvertising campaigns. Publishers should verify ad quality controls before integration.

Printful.history: Printful was founded in 2013 in Riga, Latvia (with US headquarters in Charlotte, NC) by Lauris Liberts and Davis Siksnans. It grew to become one of the largest print-on-demand and dropshipping platforms globally, processing millions of orders. In 2023, Printful merged with Printify, though both brands continued independently.
Printful.summary_abuse: Printful is a legitimate print-on-demand platform used extensively by ecommerce merchants. Minor abuse risk exists in that the platform could be used to sell counterfeit or trademark-infringing merchandise, which the company addresses via its content policies. No major fraud incidents are documented.

Printlane.history: Printlane is a Dutch digital platform offering an interactive product customizer for web-to-print businesses, enabling real-time visualization of custom print products. It targets print service providers seeking to offer online customization tools.
Printlane.summary_abuse: Printlane is a B2B web-to-print customization platform with no notable abuse signal. It operates in the print industry with no consumer-facing features commonly exploited for fraud.

Priority Hints.history: Priority Hints (later standardized as the Fetch Priority API via the fetchpriority attribute) is a web performance standard originated in the Web Incubator Community Group (WICG) around 2018. It was adopted by Chrome first, with Firefox adding support in October 2024, making it widely available in modern browsers.
Priority Hints.summary_abuse: Priority Hints is a browser performance standard with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

Prism.history: Prism.js was created by Lea Verou in 2012 as a spin-off of the Dabblet code playground. It became one of the most widely used syntax highlighting libraries on the web, appearing on millions of sites, and is maintained as a community open-source project.
Prism.summary_abuse: Prism.js has no notable abuse signal; it is a syntax highlighting JavaScript library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Prisme.history: Prisme Analytics is an open-source, privacy-focused web analytics platform (alternative to Google Analytics) written in Go and built on Grafana and ClickHouse. It is available under AGPL-3.0 for self-hosting or as a hosted SaaS. Launched around 2022.
Prisme.summary_abuse: Prisme has no notable abuse signal; it is a privacy-focused analytics tool with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

Prismic.history: Prismic was founded in 2013 in Paris, France. It has been profitable since 2016 and raised a $20 million Series A from Aglaé Ventures and Eurazeo in May 2021 to accelerate product development. It serves over 10,000 websites and specializes in Next.js and Nuxt-based headless CMS deployments.
Prismic.summary_abuse: Prismic has no notable abuse signal; it is a headless CMS serving B2B developers and marketers with no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract fraudsters.

Privado.history: Privado.ai was founded in 2020 in Zurich, Switzerland, and provides an automated privacy management platform focused on data mapping, privacy code scanning, and GDPR compliance for engineering teams. It raised seed funding and expanded into developer-focused privacy tooling.
Privado.summary_abuse: Privado.ai is a privacy compliance and data mapping platform with no abuse signal. It is a B2B compliance tool used by engineering teams and has no consumer-facing surface that could be exploited for fraud or phishing.

Privasee.history: Privasee is a UK-based GDPR compliance SaaS tool targeting small and medium-sized businesses, helping them understand their data processing activities and simplify compliance obligations. It launched as part of the growing privacy-tech sector.
Privasee.summary_abuse: Privasee is a GDPR compliance tool for SMEs with no abuse signal. As a privacy management platform, it is designed to improve data handling practices and has no features that could be exploited for fraud or phishing.

Prive.history: Prive is a subscription management platform for ecommerce brands, enabling merchants to offer subscription services and manage recurring revenue. It integrates with major ecommerce platforms including Shopify.
Prive.summary_abuse: Prive is a B2B subscription management tool for ecommerce merchants with no notable abuse signal. Its merchant-facing nature and subscription billing focus do not present common vectors for fraud or phishing.

Privia Health.history: Privia Health was founded in 2013 in Arlington, Virginia, as a physician enablement company that partners with physicians to improve clinical outcomes and financial performance. It went public on the NASDAQ in 2021 under the ticker PRVA.
Privia Health.summary_abuse: Privia Health is a publicly traded physician enablement and healthcare management platform with no abuse signal. It serves healthcare providers under HIPAA compliance requirements and has no consumer-facing surface that could be exploited for fraud.

Privy.history: Privy was founded around 2011 and evolved from a digital promotions platform into an all-in-one email marketing and conversion optimization tool for ecommerce, particularly serving Shopify merchants. It raised venture funding and grew to serve over 500,000 brands.
Privy.summary_abuse: Privy is an email and SMS marketing platform for ecommerce with moderate tracker density from its popup and conversion tools. Email marketing tools carry inherent spam risk if merchants misuse them, though Privy enforces permission-based marketing and CAN-SPAM compliance. No major abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Privy App.history: Privy App is the Shopify App Store listing of the Privy platform, an email and conversion optimization tool for ecommerce stores. It is the same product as Privy (privy.com), distributed through the Shopify ecosystem, and has accumulated a large install base among Shopify merchants.
Privy App.summary_abuse: Privy App is a Shopify-distributed email marketing and popup tool with no notable abuse signal beyond the inherent spam risk of any email marketing platform. It enforces permission-based marketing policies and CAN-SPAM compliance.

Probance.history: Probance is a French marketing automation and email personalization platform founded around 2005, with offices in Paris, Valencia, Tokyo, and Montreal. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Probance.summary_abuse: Probance is a B2B email marketing platform used by e-commerce brands. It includes mass email capabilities, but targets enterprise customers and has no documented history of notable abuse for spam or fraud.

ProcessOut.history: ProcessOut was founded in 2015 as a payment orchestration platform, and was acquired by Checkout.com in February 2020 in what was Checkout.com's first startup acquisition. The platform operates as a subsidiary, providing payment routing and analytics functionality.
ProcessOut.summary_abuse: ProcessOut has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B payment orchestration API used by merchants and payment teams, with no consumer-facing surface that would attract phishing or fraud operators.

ProcessWire.history: ProcessWire originated in 2003 as Dictator CMS. It was renamed ProcessWire in 2006 and released as open source in October 2010. Version 3.0 launched in September 2016. It is maintained by Ryan Cramer as an independent open-source project, notable for its jQuery-inspired API and developer-friendly architecture.
ProcessWire.summary_abuse: ProcessWire has a low abuse signal as an open-source CMS. Like any self-hosted CMS, unpatched or misconfigured installations could be exploited, but the platform has a strong security track record. No significant documented exploit campaigns specific to ProcessWire.

Procurios.history: Procurios has been serving associations and nonprofits in the Netherlands and Belgium for over 25 years, placing its founding around 2001. The platform offers CRM, membership management, and fundraising tools with a strong focus on GDPR compliance for European organizations.
Procurios.summary_abuse: Procurios has no notable abuse signal. It is a purpose-built CRM and membership platform for Dutch and Belgian nonprofits with no consumer-facing surface attractive to fraudsters.

ProdPad.history: ProdPad was founded in 2012 in Brighton, UK by Janna Bastow and Simon Cast, co-founders of the Mind the Product community. It remains independent and focused on product roadmap and backlog management software.
ProdPad.summary_abuse: ProdPad has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B product management SaaS used by product teams with no consumer-facing surface associated with fraud or scam campaigns.

Product Hunt.history: Product Hunt was founded by Ryan Hoover in November 2013 as a community to discover and share new tech products. In December 2016, AngelList acquired it for approximately $20 million, and it continues to operate as a subsidiary under the AngelList umbrella.
Product Hunt.summary_abuse: Product Hunt has a modest abuse signal driven by low-quality or spam product submissions, fake upvotes, and occasional listings for gray-market software. The platform moderates submissions but has limited KYC requirements. Abuse is a small fraction of overall use.

Product Personalizer.history: Product Personalizer is a Shopify app enabling merchants to offer product customization with live previews. It operates under the Zepto brand and has no notable acquisition or major ownership events documented.
Product Personalizer.summary_abuse: Product Personalizer has no notable abuse signal. It is a Shopify plugin for product customization used by e-commerce merchants with no documented history of fraud or phishing misuse.

ProductDyno.history: ProductDyno launched in 2016 and reached public beta in early 2017 as a platform for selling, licensing, and delivering digital products and memberships. It has no documented acquisition or ownership changes.
ProductDyno.summary_abuse: ProductDyno has limited abuse signal. It hosts digital products and memberships; theoretically it could be used to distribute low-quality or misleading digital content, but no specific fraud incidents have been documented publicly.

Profeat.history: Profeat is a small CRM platform integrating WhatsApp and Instagram messaging for business use. No founding year or acquisition information is publicly documented.
Profeat.summary_abuse: Profeat enables mass messaging via WhatsApp and Instagram integrations, which carries moderate abuse potential for unsolicited commercial messaging. No specific documented abuse incidents are known, but the nature of the platform warrants caution.

ProfilePress.history: ProfilePress (formerly WP User Avatar) is a WordPress membership and user registration plugin that allows site owners to create paid membership sites, sell digital products, and manage user profiles. It is available as a freemium plugin on WordPress.org.
ProfilePress.summary_abuse: ProfilePress has no notable abuse signal; it is a WordPress membership plugin installed on individual sites with no centralized consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Profit Lifter.history: Profit Lifter is a recent AI-powered platform for automated website creation, sales funnels, and email marketing. No founding year or ownership details are publicly documented.
Profit Lifter.summary_abuse: Profit Lifter provides automated landing page and funnel creation with email marketing features, which can lower barriers for spam campaigns and low-quality affiliate marketing. No specific documented incidents are known.

ProfitFlo.history: ProfitFlo is a small automation platform marketed to help businesses attract and convert customers. No founding year or ownership details are publicly documented.
ProfitFlo.summary_abuse: ProfitFlo has no notable abuse signal documented publicly. It is a small marketing automation tool with limited public presence.

Profitwell.history: ProfitWell was founded in 2012 by Patrick Campbell as a bootstrapped subscription analytics platform. It grew to 22,000 customers and $22M revenue before being acquired by UK-based Paddle for $200 million in May 2022.
Profitwell.summary_abuse: ProfitWell has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B subscription analytics platform with no consumer-facing surface that would attract phishing or fraud operators.

Profity.history: Profity is an e-commerce cross-promotion network enabling partner shops to market offers to customers post-purchase. No founding year or acquisition information is publicly available.
Profity.summary_abuse: Profity enables cross-shop post-purchase marketing offers, which can be misused for deceptive upsells or unwanted promotions. No specific documented fraud incidents are known.

Programia.history: Programia s.r.o. was founded around 2013 and is headquartered in Ostrava, Czech Republic. It specialises in custom B2C and B2B ecommerce systems integrated with ERP, CRM, accounting, and logistics platforms; no notable ownership changes or deprecation events since founding.
Programia.summary_abuse: Programia is a B2B ecommerce development platform with no notable abuse signal. It builds custom enterprise shops rather than hosting consumer-facing storefronts directly, meaning fraudsters have no meaningful surface to exploit. No documented incidents found.

Progreda.history: Progreda is a small sales automation platform enabling automated conversations via SMS, Instagram, and Facebook Messenger. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Progreda.summary_abuse: Progreda facilitates automated messaging across SMS and social platforms, which can be misused for unsolicited mass messaging or spam campaigns. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Progress MOVEit.history: MOVEit was originally developed by Ipswitch and acquired by Progress Software in 2019. In May 2023, a critical zero-day SQL injection vulnerability (CVE-2023-34362) was exploited by the Cl0p ransomware group, leading to breaches at hundreds of organizations including government agencies, financial institutions, and healthcare providers worldwide — one of the largest mass-exploitation events in cybersecurity history.
Progress MOVEit.summary_abuse: MOVEit itself is a legitimate enterprise file transfer product, not an abuse tool. However, the May 2023 CVE-2023-34362 SQL injection zero-day was mass-exploited by the Cl0p ransomware gang, resulting in data breaches at hundreds of organizations. The vulnerability was in the MOVEit Transfer server software and represented a significant supply-chain style attack on enterprise MFT infrastructure. Progress issued patches promptly and published detailed guidance, but the scale of exploitation was exceptional. MOVEit remains widely deployed for sensitive regulated-data transfer.

Progress Sitefinity.history: Progress Sitefinity was first released around 2007 by Telerik, a Bulgarian software company. Progress Software acquired Telerik in 2014 and rebranded the CMS as Progress Sitefinity. It is built on ASP.NET and targets mid-to-large enterprise web publishing use cases.
Progress Sitefinity.summary_abuse: Progress Sitefinity is an enterprise .NET CMS with no notable direct abuse signal. Sites built on Sitefinity can be targeted like any CMS if left unpatched, but the product itself is not commonly associated with phishing hosting or fraud infrastructure. Progress Software publishes security advisories and patches routinely.

Progress WS_FTP.history: WS_FTP was originally developed by Ipswitch in the early 1990s and became one of the most widely used FTP clients on Windows. Progress Software acquired Ipswitch in 2019 and now maintains both the WS_FTP client and server products. In October 2023, Progress disclosed critical vulnerabilities in WS_FTP Server (CVE-2023-40044), which were quickly weaponised by threat actors.
Progress WS_FTP.summary_abuse: WS_FTP Server had a critical authentication bypass vulnerability (CVE-2023-40044) disclosed in October 2023 that was actively exploited within days of public disclosure. Attackers used it to drop webshells and ransomware on vulnerable servers. Progress patched promptly and issued emergency advisories. The product is a legitimate enterprise file transfer tool, and abuse stems from exploitation of unpatched installations rather than inherent service design.

ProgressBar.js.history: ProgressBar.js is a small open-source JavaScript library created by Kimmo Brunfeldt around 2014 for rendering responsive animated SVG progress bars; it has no commercial backing and has had minimal updates in recent years.
ProgressBar.js.summary_abuse: ProgressBar.js is a small client-side SVG animation library with no abuse signal; it has no consumer-facing surface and no features that fraudsters would target.

Project Wonderful.history: Project Wonderful was a webcomic-focused online advertising marketplace founded in 2006 by Ryan North. It used a real-time auction model for ad placements and was popular among webcomic creators and independent publishers. The service was shut down in August 2018 when North announced it was no longer financially sustainable.
Project Wonderful.summary_abuse: Project Wonderful was a niche ad marketplace primarily used by webcomic creators; it shut down in 2018. During its operation it had no notable abuse signal relative to its size. The platform is now deprecated and no longer accessible.

Projesoft.history: Projesoft is a Turkish software development company offering custom IT solutions including e-commerce platforms and ERP systems. No specific founding year or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
Projesoft.summary_abuse: Projesoft has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Turkish IT services company with no consumer-facing surface associated with fraud or phishing.

Prolo Finder.history: Prolo Finder is a small SaaS tool providing embeddable store locator functionality for websites. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Prolo Finder.summary_abuse: Prolo Finder has no notable abuse signal. It is a simple store locator widget with no features that would attract fraud or phishing operators.

Prom.history: Prom.ua is a Ukrainian online marketplace founded around 2008, serving as a platform for businesses and consumers to buy and sell goods in Ukraine. The company has grown to become one of Ukraine's leading B2B and B2C ecommerce platforms.
Prom.summary_abuse: Prom.ua is an open consumer marketplace which, like any general-purpose trading platform, can host fraudulent seller accounts, fake product listings, and counterfeit goods. The primary abuse vectors are fake storefronts and non-delivery scams common to open marketplaces. The platform has a buyer-protection policy but enforcement consistency is unclear from public sources.

Promio.history: Promio is a US-based marketing platform targeting businesses and franchise networks, providing tools for customer engagement and brand visibility. No specific founding year or acquisition history is publicly documented.
Promio.summary_abuse: Promio has limited abuse signal. It is a B2B marketing platform for franchises with mass-email capabilities, but targets established businesses with no documented history of enabling spam or fraud at scale.

Prommt.history: Prommt is an Irish payment request SaaS founded around 2017, focused on helping businesses collect card and bank transfer payments through digital request links; no notable ownership changes found.
Prommt.summary_abuse: Prommt is a payment request platform used by legitimate businesses to collect payments. As with any payment-related tool, its payment request links could theoretically be misused for phishing, but the platform requires business onboarding and is subject to PCI-DSS controls, limiting abuse potential. No documented fraud incidents found.

Promo AI.history: Promo.com (marketed as Promo AI) is an Israeli-founded AI video creation and scheduling platform. It has grown as a VC-backed company primarily serving small business marketing teams.
Promo AI.summary_abuse: Promo AI has minimal documented abuse signal. It is a video marketing creation platform primarily used by legitimate small businesses and marketers.

PromoBuilding.history: PromoBuilding is a Russian subscription-based website builder for promotional campaigns. No specific founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
PromoBuilding.summary_abuse: PromoBuilding is a Russian-hosted website builder that allows creation of promotional campaign sites. Russian-hosted website builders carry elevated risk of hosting deceptive or fraudulent campaign pages. No specific incidents documented.

PromoTix.history: PromoTix was founded in 2019 by Will Royall and is headquartered in Christiansted, US Virgin Islands. The company offers a ticketing and event marketing platform with a pay-as-you-go fee model and raised seed funding, including equity crowdfunding via Wefunder. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are recorded.
PromoTix.summary_abuse: PromoTix is a ticketing platform with a free tier and pay-as-you-go pricing. Like all event ticketing platforms, it carries some risk of fraudulent event listings or ticket scalping, but no documented abuse incidents specific to PromoTix have been reported. The platform requires event organiser accounts and payment processing integration, which limits fully anonymous misuse.

PRONOTE.history: PRONOTE is a French school information system developed by Index Education, a company founded in 1994. It is deployed in approximately 7,400 French schools and provides administrative, teaching, and family communication tools. It is the dominant school management platform in France.
PRONOTE.summary_abuse: PRONOTE has no notable abuse signal. It is a French school information system used by educational institutions; its closed, institution-licensed model means it has no open consumer-facing surface that fraud operators would target.

Proof Factor.history: Proof Factor was founded in 2020 in Ann Arbor, Michigan as a social proof and gamified popup tool for e-commerce conversion optimization. No notable acquisition or ownership changes documented.
Proof Factor.summary_abuse: Proof Factor displays live visitor counts and purchase activity popups, a pattern sometimes used to create false urgency or inflate perceived popularity on e-commerce sites. This dark-pattern risk is moderate. No specific external fraud incidents are documented.

Proofdy.history: Proofdy is a small social proof popup tool for websites. No founding year or ownership details are publicly documented.
Proofdy.summary_abuse: Proofdy embeds social proof popups that can be used to display fabricated or exaggerated activity, which is a known dark pattern in e-commerce. No specific fraud incidents are documented.

Prooven.history: Prooven is a small conversion and trust-building platform for websites. No founding year or ownership details are publicly documented.
Prooven.summary_abuse: Prooven provides trust and credibility signals for websites; if misused, these features could generate fake social proof. No specific fraud incidents are documented.

Propcart.history: Propcart is an e-commerce platform for prop rental houses enabling inventory management and online transactions. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Propcart.summary_abuse: Propcart has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche platform for prop house inventory and rentals with no documented association with fraud.

Property Shell.history: Property Shell is an Australian CRM platform for property development and project marketing, headquartered in Melbourne, Victoria. The company serves over 50,000 properties and is run by a small team of around 9 employees. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are recorded.
Property Shell.summary_abuse: Property Shell is a B2B CRM platform for the property development sector with no notable abuse signal. Its closed professional deployment model and B2B customer base mean it is not exploited for phishing or consumer-facing fraud.

PropFlo.history: PropFlo is an AI-powered real estate CRM from India targeted at property developers and channel partners. No founding year or major ownership changes are publicly documented.
PropFlo.summary_abuse: PropFlo has no notable abuse signal. It is a real estate CRM used by property developers with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud or phishing.

Proposify.history: Proposify was founded in 2012 in Halifax, Canada, and has grown to serve 10,000+ customers generating $11M in revenue. The company is SOC2 Type 2 certified and remains independently operated.
Proposify.summary_abuse: Proposify has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B proposal and contract management platform used by sales teams and agencies, with no consumer-facing surface associated with fraud.

ProProfs Quiz Maker.history: ProProfs launched its quiz platform in 2005 as part of the broader ProProfs suite of online learning and knowledge-management tools. The company is US-based and has grown to serve over 50 million quiz takers without notable acquisition or ownership changes.
ProProfs Quiz Maker.summary_abuse: ProProfs Quiz Maker has no notable abuse signal. It is primarily used by educators and businesses for legitimate assessments and training, and has no documented history of being leveraged for phishing or scam campaigns.

ProSites.history: Founded in 2003 in the US, ProSites pioneered CMS-based website design for dental and medical professionals, reportedly serving over 7,500 dental practices with no notable acquisition or ownership changes.
ProSites.summary_abuse: ProSites has no notable abuse signal. It is a purpose-built website platform for dental and medical practices, with a highly targeted professional client base and no documented association with phishing or fraud.

Prospa.history: Prospa was founded in December 2011 by Beau Bertoli and Gregory Moshal as an online lender for Australian small businesses. It listed on the ASX in June 2019 raising A$110 million, then was taken private in 2024 by a consortium led by Salter Brothers Tech Fund for approximately A$74 million, well below its IPO price.
Prospa.summary_abuse: Prospa has no notable abuse signal as a technology. It is a regulated Australian fintech lender subject to financial services oversight, with no documented use in phishing or fraud infrastructure.

Prosperous AI.history: Prosperous AI is a small AI-powered messaging automation platform for local businesses. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Prosperous AI.summary_abuse: Prosperous AI enables automated customer messaging which could be misused for mass-messaging spam. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Prostoy.history: Prostoy is a Russian CRM system for small and medium-sized businesses. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Prostoy.summary_abuse: Prostoy has no notable abuse signal. It is a Russian CRM platform for SMBs with no documented use in phishing or fraud campaigns.

Proticaret.history: Proticaret is one of Turkey's earliest free ecommerce software platforms, built by Turkish developers using ASP/.NET. It has been described as Turkey's first free ecommerce solution and caters primarily to small Turkish online retailers.
Proticaret.summary_abuse: Proticaret is a Turkish ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a locally-focused solution for Turkish small businesses with no documented history of exploitation for fraud or malicious hosting.

Proto AICX.history: Proto AICX is a multilingual AI customer experience automation platform serving businesses and governments with chatbots and contact center solutions. No specific founding year or major ownership events are publicly documented.
Proto AICX.summary_abuse: Proto AICX has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B/B2G AI contact center platform with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.

Proton Mail.history: Proton Mail was founded in 2013 by scientists at CERN in Geneva, Switzerland, including Andy Yen, Jason Stockman, and Wei Sun, who developed end-to-end encrypted email to protect scientific communications. The service launched publicly in 2014 and grew to over 70 million users by 2024. The parent company Proton AG later expanded into a broader privacy ecosystem including Proton VPN, Proton Drive, and Proton Calendar.
Proton Mail.summary_abuse: Proton Mail's free tier and strong anonymity guarantees make it occasionally attractive for abuse, including use of proton.me addresses in phishing and spam campaigns. However, Proton AG publishes a transparency report, cooperates with lawful authorities under Swiss law, and has a documented abuse-reporting process. The platform applies rate limiting and anti-abuse detection. Abuse is a small fraction of overall use given the 70 million user base, and Proton is widely considered privacy-first rather than anonymity-for-abuse.

Prototype.history: Prototype.js was created by Sam Stephenson in 2005, initially as part of Ruby on Rails to simplify JavaScript DOM manipulation and Ajax in the early Web 2.0 era. It was once one of the most widely used JavaScript frameworks before jQuery and later modern frameworks displaced it. Development has been effectively abandoned since around 2010-2012, with no meaningful releases since.
Prototype.summary_abuse: Prototype.js has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend-integrated JavaScript library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Protovis.history: Protovis was created by Mike Bostock and Jeff Heer at Stanford University around 2009 as a JavaScript data visualization library. It was deprecated in favor of D3.js, which Bostock created as a successor and which became the dominant web data visualization library.
Protovis.summary_abuse: Protovis has no notable abuse signal. It is a deprecated JavaScript data visualization library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Provely.history: Provely is a US-based social proof conversion tool displaying verified activity on websites. No founding year or major ownership events are publicly documented.
Provely.summary_abuse: Provely displays live visitor and purchase activity popups, which can be configured to show inflated or fabricated engagement numbers, a recognized dark pattern used to deceive prospective buyers. No specific phishing incidents are documented, but the feature design enables deceptive practices.

ProvenExpert.history: ProvenExpert is a German review aggregation and marketing platform founded around 2011. It allows businesses to collect and display customer reviews and ratings for marketing purposes.
ProvenExpert.summary_abuse: ProvenExpert has limited abuse potential as a review marketing platform. Fake review generation is a known pattern on review aggregation platforms; however, no specific ProvenExpert fraud incidents are publicly documented.

ProveSource.history: ProveSource was founded in 2018 as a social proof notification platform, displaying recent activity and conversion events on websites to boost visitor trust and conversion rates.
ProveSource.summary_abuse: ProveSource displays social proof widgets (recent purchases, sign-ups, visitor counts) on websites. It has no notable abuse signal for phishing or malware, but social proof widgets of this kind are sometimes used on fake storefronts to lend false legitimacy to scam shopping sites; the vendor has no documented anti-abuse posture beyond standard terms of service.

Provide Support.history: Provide Support is a live chat and real-time website monitoring SaaS that has been active since the early 2000s. No notable ownership changes or major events are publicly documented.
Provide Support.summary_abuse: Provide Support has no notable abuse signal. It is a live chat customer service tool with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud or phishing.

Proximis.history: Proximis was created in 2010 in Strasbourg, France as a unified commerce SaaS platform for retail. The company was later acquired by Planet, a global payments and commerce company.
Proximis.summary_abuse: Proximis has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B unified commerce platform for retail with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.

Proximis Unified Commerce.history: Proximis Unified Commerce is the flagship product of French SaaS company Proximis, founded in 2010 in Strasbourg. The software has been actively marketed since 2017 and the company was acquired by Planet, a global payments company.
Proximis Unified Commerce.summary_abuse: Proximis Unified Commerce has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise retail commerce platform with no consumer-facing surface associated with fraud.

Proxmox Mail Gateway.history: Proxmox Mail Gateway is developed by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH, an Austrian company founded in 2005 in Vienna. It is an open-source email security gateway built on Debian Linux, providing spam filtering, virus protection, and email quarantine. The product is freely usable under AGPL-3.0 with commercial support subscriptions available from Proxmox.
Proxmox Mail Gateway.summary_abuse: Proxmox Mail Gateway has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted email security appliance run by system administrators to filter spam and malware, not a service that fraudsters can exploit for phishing or content hosting.

Proxmox VE.history: Proxmox VE was first released in 2007 by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH, an Austrian company headquartered in Vienna. It combines KVM hypervisor and LXC container technology with a web-based management interface and is one of the leading open-source virtualization platforms for on-premises server workloads. It remains independently owned and has grown substantially in the enterprise and service-provider market.
Proxmox VE.summary_abuse: Proxmox VE has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted virtualization platform deployed by system administrators and data center operators; it has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would exploit.

PSPad.history: PSPad is a freeware text editor for Windows developed by Czech developer Jan Fiala, available since the early 2000s. It provides syntax highlighting for many programming languages and has been a popular lightweight code editor in the Central European developer community.
PSPad.summary_abuse: PSPad has no notable abuse signal. It is a desktop text editor for Windows with no network-connected features that would attract fraud operators.

Psyma.history: Psyma is a behavior analytics platform for the automotive industry, providing data-driven insights to automotive brands and dealers. No founding year or ownership details are publicly documented.
Psyma.summary_abuse: Psyma has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche automotive analytics platform with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.

Ptengine.history: Ptengine was founded around 2010 in Tokyo, Japan, launching its heatmap and web analytics platform in 2013. It was notable for being an early provider of mobile-responsive heatmaps and grew to over 200,000 users globally.
Ptengine.summary_abuse: Ptengine is a web analytics and heatmap tool with no notable abuse signal; it operates as a backend analytics SDK and does not expose consumer-facing hosting or messaging features that would be attractive for phishing or fraud.

Pterodactyl Panel.history: Pterodactyl Panel is a free, open-source game server management panel created around 2015. It is built with PHP (Laravel), React, and Go, and has become a widely used panel in the game hosting community for managing Minecraft, CS:GO, and other game servers. It is maintained by community contributors with no commercial backing.
Pterodactyl Panel.summary_abuse: Pterodactyl Panel is occasionally used in the game-hosting piracy ecosystem, where unauthorized game server providers use it to run unlicensed game servers at scale. However, this is a marginal concern and the vast majority of its use is legitimate. The software itself has no consumer-facing surface for phishing or fraud.

PTminder.history: PTminder is an Australian business management SaaS platform designed specifically for personal trainers and fitness professionals, providing scheduling, client management, and payment tools.
PTminder.summary_abuse: PTminder has no notable abuse signal. It is a business management platform for personal trainers with a narrow vertical focus and no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract fraud operators.

Pubble.history: Pubble is an Irish customer messaging platform for teams to interact with customers via live chat and messaging. No founding year or major ownership events are publicly documented.
Pubble.summary_abuse: Pubble has no notable abuse signal. It is a customer messaging platform with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud or phishing.

Pubfunnels.history: Pubfunnels is a CRM and business management SaaS hub designed for publishers, authors, and media professionals. It is a small, recent product with limited public history.
Pubfunnels.summary_abuse: Pubfunnels is a CRM tool for publishers with no notable abuse signal. It is a niche business management product with no documented history of exploitation by bad actors.

PubGuru.history: PubGuru is the header bidding and ad ops platform built by MonetizeMore, a Canadian ad tech company founded around 2010. MonetizeMore grew to $20M+ annual revenue as a publisher monetization intermediary.
PubGuru.summary_abuse: PubGuru is an ad tech platform that injects multiple third-party trackers and ad scripts onto publisher pages. Ad tech infrastructure is commonly exploited for malvertising; no specific PubGuru incidents are documented but the category carries inherent elevated risk.

Public CMS.history: Public CMS is an open-source content management system for building and managing websites. No specific founding year or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
Public CMS.summary_abuse: Public CMS has modest abuse potential as a self-hosted CMS that can be configured to host arbitrary content. No specific documented fraud incidents are known.

Publii.history: Publii is a free, open-source desktop static site generator developed by TidyHQ / Tidycms team, launched around 2018. It is privacy-focused and generates static websites without requiring a server, making it popular among bloggers and small publishers who prioritize privacy.
Publii.summary_abuse: Publii has no notable abuse signal. It is a desktop-based static site generator that outputs pure HTML; it has no server infrastructure, content hosting, or anonymous sign-up features that fraudsters would target.

PublishPress Blocks.history: PublishPress Blocks is a WordPress Gutenberg block editor plugin by the PublishPress team, adding extended block types for content editors. PublishPress is a company focused on WordPress editorial workflow tools.
PublishPress Blocks.summary_abuse: PublishPress Blocks has no notable abuse signal. It is a WordPress page builder plugin with no independent hosting or user-facing infrastructure that fraudsters would exploit.

Publishrr.history: Publishrr was founded around 2006 and is a website management and monetisation platform developed by Bramerz Private Limited, a Pakistani software company. It targets newspaper, TV channel, magazine, and radio station websites, providing CMS and ad monetisation tools.
Publishrr.summary_abuse: Publishrr is a niche CMS platform for media publishers with no documented abuse signal. Its primary use is by legitimate media organisations for website management, and there are no public reports of it being exploited for phishing or spam.

PubLive.history: PubLive is a headless CMS for online publishers, likely founded in India. No specific founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
PubLive.summary_abuse: PubLive has no notable abuse signal. It is a headless CMS for online publishers with no documented use in phishing or fraud.

Publive.history: Publive is an Indian headless CMS for online publishers. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Publive.summary_abuse: Publive has no notable abuse signal. It is a headless CMS for publishers with no documented use in phishing or fraud.

Publy.history: PUBLY was founded in 2015 in South Korea as a digital content subscription platform targeting working professionals with curated reports on career development, industry trends, and business topics. The platform primarily serves a Korean-speaking audience and has received venture capital funding. No major acquisitions or rebrands are recorded.
Publy.summary_abuse: PUBLY is a Korean subscription content platform with no notable abuse signal. It operates a closed, subscription-based model for professional content and is not associated with phishing, spam, or fraudulent activity.

Publytics.history: Publytics is a cookieless, privacy-first web analytics alternative to Google Analytics designed for online publishers. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Publytics.summary_abuse: Publytics has no notable abuse signal. It is a privacy-focused analytics tool for publishers with no features that would attract fraud operators.

PubMatic.history: PubMatic was founded in 2006 by Rajeev Goel and Amar Goel in Redwood City, California as a sell-side programmatic advertising platform. It went public on NASDAQ in December 2020 under the ticker PUBM. It serves as a key infrastructure player in the open programmatic advertising ecosystem.
PubMatic.summary_abuse: PubMatic operates within the programmatic advertising ecosystem, which has documented ad fraud, malvertising, and domain spoofing challenges industry-wide. PubMatic has implemented IAB ads.txt and sellers.json compliance and participates in anti-fraud initiatives. The platform itself is not a primary abuse vector but its pipes can carry malvertising if upstream supply chain controls fail.

PubNub.history: PubNub was founded in 2010 in San Francisco and provides a real-time data streaming network (pub/sub messaging, presence, push notifications) for developers building connected applications. It has raised multiple rounds of venture funding and serves large enterprise customers including healthcare, gaming, and financial services verticals.
PubNub.summary_abuse: PubNub has limited abuse signal. As a real-time messaging infrastructure provider, it could theoretically be used for malware C2 communications, but this is not a documented pattern specific to PubNub. The platform requires API keys for access, limiting anonymous misuse.

Pubperf.history: Pubperf is an analytics SaaS for digital publishers, providing audience and advertising performance metrics. It is a niche product with limited public history.
Pubperf.summary_abuse: Pubperf is a publisher analytics tool with no notable abuse signal. It is used by digital media companies to monitor content and ad performance and has no documented history of exploitation.

Pubstack.history: Pubstack is a French ad tech company providing real-time analytics and revenue optimization tools for digital publishers. No founding year or acquisition history is publicly documented.
Pubstack.summary_abuse: Pubstack is an ad monetization platform that embeds multiple ad tech trackers on publisher pages. Ad tech infrastructure carries inherent malvertising risk; no specific Pubstack incidents are documented.

PubSubJS.history: PubSubJS is a lightweight topic-based publish/subscribe library for JavaScript, created by Morgan Roderick around 2010 and published on GitHub under the MIT license. It remains a small community-maintained utility with no commercial backing.
PubSubJS.summary_abuse: PubSubJS has no notable abuse signal. It is a small client-side JavaScript library for event-based messaging with no network or server surface that fraudsters would target.

PubTech.history: PubTech is a consent management platform helping brands collect and manage customer consents, likely based in France. No founding year or acquisition history is publicly documented.
PubTech.summary_abuse: PubTech has no notable abuse signal. It is a consent management platform focused on GDPR compliance with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.

PukiWiki.history: PukiWiki is a Japanese open-source wiki engine created around 2001, heavily popular in Japan during the early 2000s web era. Development has largely stalled and the project is now dormant, having been superseded by more modern wiki platforms.
PukiWiki.summary_abuse: PukiWiki has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted wiki engine with limited deployment. Old, unmaintained wiki installations can become targets for spam comments but this is not a characteristic specific to PukiWiki.

Pulse CMS.history: Pulse CMS was founded on 1 January 2009 as a lightweight flat-file content management system requiring no database. It has operated as an independent small/solo product, offering perpetual licenses for individuals, freelancers, and agencies.
Pulse CMS.summary_abuse: Pulse CMS has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted flat-file CMS with no cloud hosting component, giving the vendor no surface for fraud or phishing infrastructure.

Pulse Insights.history: Pulse Insights was founded around 2012 by Jeremy Bieger and is headquartered in New York City, New York. The company provides an AI-powered customer feedback platform that embeds contextual micro-surveys into digital experiences to help enterprises collect and activate customer insights. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are recorded.
Pulse Insights.summary_abuse: Pulse Insights is a B2B customer feedback survey platform with no notable abuse signal. Its enterprise deployment model and lack of anonymous public access mean it is not associated with phishing, spam, or fraudulent activity.

Pulse Secure.history: Pulse Secure was formed in 2014 when Siris Capital acquired the Junos Pulse SSL VPN business from Juniper Networks. Ivanti acquired Pulse Secure in December 2020 alongside MobileIron. The product line has since been rebranded under the Ivanti Secure Access family.
Pulse Secure.summary_abuse: Pulse Secure VPN infrastructure has been a target of state-sponsored attackers exploiting zero-day vulnerabilities; in 2021 CISA warned of widespread exploitation of Pulse Connect Secure. The product itself is a legitimate enterprise VPN, but the vulnerabilities have been exploited by nation-state actors for credential theft and network intrusion.

PulseM.history: PulseM is a customer review and reputation management platform for home service businesses that integrated with ServiceTitan in 2017. No specific founding year or acquisition history is publicly documented.
PulseM.summary_abuse: PulseM has no notable abuse signal. It is a customer feedback and review management tool for home service businesses with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.

punBB.history: PunBB was created in 2003 by Swedish developer Rickard Andersson as a lightweight PHP forum alternative. In 2007, Informer Technologies acquired development rights. Andersson stepped back in 2008, causing a team split; FluxBB was forked at that time. The last stable release was 1.4.6 in March 2021, fixing XSS vulnerabilities.
punBB.summary_abuse: PunBB has no notable abuse signal as a platform vendor. It is a self-hosted forum software. Individual forum instances may be subject to spam and registration abuse, but this depends on the operator's configuration rather than any characteristic of PunBB itself.

Punchpass.history: Punchpass is a class management and scheduling system for fitness studios, yoga, and wellness businesses. No specific founding year or notable ownership changes are publicly documented.
Punchpass.summary_abuse: Punchpass has no notable abuse signal. It is a class management tool for fitness businesses with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.

PunchTab.history: PunchTab was founded in 2011 by Ranjith Kumaran and Mehdi Ait Oufkir as a loyalty and customer engagement platform for CPG and retail brands. WalmartLabs acquired PunchTab in September 2015 to enhance Sam's Club customer engagement capabilities.
PunchTab.summary_abuse: PunchTab has no notable abuse signal. It was a loyalty and engagement platform acquired by Walmart for enterprise use, with no documented use in fraud or phishing campaigns.

punycode.history: punycode is a JavaScript library for encoding/decoding internationalized domain names (IDNs) using Punycode. It was bundled in Node.js core and is widely used for IDNA processing.
punycode.summary_abuse: punycode has no notable abuse signal; it is a domain name encoding utility library.

PuppetVendors.history: PuppetVendors is a Shopify-integrated SaaS for managing vendor sales, commissions, and payouts. It is a niche ecommerce management product with limited public history.
PuppetVendors.summary_abuse: PuppetVendors is a Shopify vendor management tool with no notable abuse signal. It handles commission and payout reporting for ecommerce sellers and has no documented history of exploitation.

Pure Chat.history: Pure Chat is a live chat SaaS for small and mid-sized businesses founded around 2012. No notable acquisition or major ownership changes are documented.
Pure Chat.summary_abuse: Pure Chat has no notable abuse signal. It is a live chat tool for small businesses with no features associated with phishing or fraud infrastructure.

Pure CSS.history: Pure CSS was created by Yahoo in 2013 as a lightweight, modular CSS framework. It was open-sourced under the BSD license. While Yahoo has undergone multiple ownership changes (acquired by Verizon, then by private equity firm Apollo Global Management as part of Yahoo Inc.), Pure CSS remains an open-source project on GitHub.
Pure CSS.summary_abuse: Pure CSS has no notable abuse signal. It is a CSS framework library with no network surface or consumer-facing infrastructure.

PureCars.history: PureCars is a US automotive software and managed services company serving dealerships, OEMs, and advertising associations. No specific founding year or notable acquisition history is publicly documented.
PureCars.summary_abuse: PureCars has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B automotive advertising and analytics platform used by dealerships and OEMs with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.

PureClarity.history: PureClarity was founded in 2014 in York, United Kingdom, as an AI-driven ecommerce personalization platform. In 2019 it raised a £750K investment from Mercia to accelerate product development.
PureClarity.summary_abuse: PureClarity is an ecommerce personalization and product recommendation platform with no notable abuse signal; it operates as a backend recommendation engine and does not provide consumer-facing hosting features exploitable for phishing or fraud.

PurpleAds.history: PurpleAds is an advertising network connecting publishers and advertisers, serving in-page push ads, display banners, and floating ad formats. It caters to both small publishers and advertisers looking for Google Search and display network reach.
PurpleAds.summary_abuse: PurpleAds is an advertising network with moderate abuse potential typical of smaller ad networks. Ad networks can be exploited to serve malvertising or low-quality ads, and smaller networks may have less rigorous advertiser vetting than major platforms. No specific high-profile malvertising incidents are documented, but the platform's open publisher model and diverse ad formats (including push and floating ads) are formats commonly associated with nuisance and intrusive advertising.

PushAd.history: PushAd is a Polish push notification platform for websites. No founding year or acquisition information is publicly documented.
PushAd.summary_abuse: PushAd enables browser push notifications which are commonly abused for spam campaigns and misleading notification subscriptions. Polish push notification vendors have appeared in abuse reports for deceptive subscription prompts. No specific documented incidents are known for PushAd.

PushAlert.history: PushAlert is an Indian multi-channel push notification and onsite messaging platform. No specific founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
PushAlert.summary_abuse: PushAlert enables web push notifications including a free tier, which is commonly leveraged for spam campaigns and deceptive subscription prompts. Push notification services generally carry moderate abuse risk from notification spam. No specific PushAlert fraud incidents are documented.

PushBots.history: PushBots was founded in 2012 in Cairo, Egypt as a web and mobile push notification platform. No notable acquisition or ownership changes are publicly documented.
PushBots.summary_abuse: PushBots offers a free tier for push notification delivery, which is a common vector for notification spam campaigns. Push notification services with free tiers are regularly used by abusers to send unwanted subscription-based spam. No specific PushBots fraud incidents are documented.

PushDaddy Whatsapp Chat.history: PushDaddy is a small Indian platform offering WhatsApp live chat and abandoned cart recovery for e-commerce. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
PushDaddy Whatsapp Chat.summary_abuse: PushDaddy integrates WhatsApp messaging for e-commerce, which carries moderate abuse potential for unsolicited messaging. No specific documented incidents are known.

Pushe.history: Pushe is an Iranian push notification and app analytics platform for mobile and web, based in Tehran. No specific founding year or acquisition information is publicly documented.
Pushe.summary_abuse: Pushe is an Iranian push notification provider. Push notification services are regularly abused for spam campaigns. The Iranian jurisdiction and limited compliance posture elevate the risk profile beyond typical Western providers.

PushEngage.history: PushEngage is a US-based browser push notification platform for content websites and e-commerce. No notable acquisition or major ownership changes are publicly documented.
PushEngage.summary_abuse: PushEngage provides web push notification capabilities including a free tier. Push notification services are routinely exploited for spam and deceptive subscription prompts. No specific PushEngage incidents are documented, but the category inherently carries moderate abuse risk.

Pusher.history: Pusher was founded in 2010 in London by Max Williams and Damien Tanner, providing hosted APIs for real-time WebSocket connections. It grew to become a leading real-time messaging infrastructure platform and was acquired by MessageBird in 2020.
Pusher.summary_abuse: Pusher has limited abuse signal. As a real-time communication infrastructure provider, it could theoretically be used as a C2 channel by malware, but this is not a documented pattern specific to Pusher. The platform requires API key authentication limiting anonymous misuse.

Pushify.history: Pushify is a web push notification SaaS that delivers browser-based push notifications across desktop and mobile. It operates in a category that is commonly used for legitimate re-engagement but also for spam and notification abuse.
Pushify.summary_abuse: Pushify is a push notification service with moderate abuse potential. Web push notification platforms are frequently exploited to serve unsolicited notifications, spam, and scam landing pages, since users can be tricked into subscribing. The free tier with low barriers to entry increases risk. No specific high-profile incidents are documented for Pushify specifically, but the category is broadly associated with notification spam.

Pushly.history: Pushly is a web push notification platform for digital publishers and media companies. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Pushly.summary_abuse: Pushly is a web push notification platform targeting publishers, which is a common vector for notification spam. No specific fraud incidents are documented.
