Pushnami.history: Pushnami is an AI-powered push notification and email messaging platform. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Pushnami.summary_abuse: Pushnami provides push, social, and email messaging capabilities. Cross-channel mass-messaging platforms carry moderate abuse potential for spam. No specific Pushnami fraud incidents are documented.

Pushouse.history: Pushouse is a marketing automation SaaS focused on ecommerce sales enhancement through data-driven automation. It is a small product with minimal public history.
Pushouse.summary_abuse: Pushouse is an ecommerce marketing automation tool with no notable abuse signal. It is a niche product with no documented history of exploitation for spam or fraud.

PushOwl.history: PushOwl is an Indian web push notification platform for Shopify e-commerce stores, focused on abandoned cart recovery and marketing automation. No notable acquisition or ownership changes are documented.
PushOwl.summary_abuse: PushOwl enables web push notifications for Shopify stores. While primarily legitimate, web push notification platforms can be misused for spam campaigns. No specific PushOwl fraud incidents are documented.

PushOwl Web Push Notifications.history: PushOwl Web Push Notifications is the Shopify-specific product variant of PushOwl, an Indian push notification platform for e-commerce abandoned cart recovery. No notable ownership changes are documented.
PushOwl Web Push Notifications.summary_abuse: PushOwl Web Push Notifications is a Shopify push notification app. While primarily used for legitimate e-commerce, web push notifications can be misused for unsolicited marketing. No specific fraud incidents are documented.

Pushpad.history: Pushpad is an Italian push notification service founded around 2014, designed for sending browser notifications from websites and web apps; no notable ownership changes or major events are documented.
Pushpad.summary_abuse: Pushpad is a browser push notification service. Push notification channels can be used for spam or phishing if misused, but Pushpad is a smaller developer-focused tool and has no specific documented abuse incidents.

Pushpay.history: Pushpay was founded in 2011 in New Zealand by Chris Heaslip and Eliot Crowther as a digital giving and engagement platform for churches and non-profits. It listed on the New Zealand Stock Exchange (NZX) and later also on the ASX. In 2023, it was taken private by a consortium including BGH Capital.
Pushpay.summary_abuse: Pushpay has limited abuse signal. It processes donations for churches and non-profits and is PCI-DSS certified. As a payment and engagement platform, it could theoretically be targeted by social engineering attacks against churches, but Pushpay itself is a legitimate PCI-compliant service.

PushPushGo.history: PushPushGo is a Polish GDPR-ready web push notification platform serving startups, SMBs, and corporations for e-commerce and content engagement use cases. No notable acquisition or ownership changes are documented.
PushPushGo.summary_abuse: PushPushGo provides web push notifications with a free tier, which is a common vector for notification spam. The GDPR focus and enterprise customer base suggest better anti-abuse posture than smaller providers, but the category carries inherent moderate risk.

Pushub.history: Pushub is a self-serve demand-side platform (DSP) targeting affiliates and agencies, serving push notification and pop-up ad formats. It operates in the performance marketing / affiliate ad space.
Pushub.summary_abuse: Pushub is a DSP serving affiliates and agencies with push and pop-up ad formats, a category frequently associated with malvertising, misleading ads, and aggressive notification spam. Self-serve affiliate DSPs with minimal vetting have elevated abuse potential because they are favored by low-quality advertisers. No specific high-profile incidents are documented for Pushub, but the platform targets the affiliate marketing segment which has a documented history of fraud, misleading offers, and click fraud.

PushWoosh.history: PushWoosh is a push notification and in-app messaging platform for mobile and web, founded around 2011. It serves a broad range of businesses globally. No notable acquisition or major ownership changes are documented.
PushWoosh.summary_abuse: PushWoosh provides mobile and web push notifications with a free tier. Push notification infrastructure is routinely exploited for spam campaigns and deceptive subscription prompts. No specific PushWoosh fraud incidents are documented, but the category carries inherent moderate abuse risk.

Puter.js.history: Puter.js is a JavaScript library for serverless auth in browser, first released around 2023 as an open-source project.
Puter.js.summary_abuse: Puter.js has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer-focused open-source library with no consumer-facing infrastructure.

PWA.history: Progressive Web Apps (PWA) is a set of web platform capabilities and best practices, coined by Google's Alex Russell and Frances Berriman in 2015. The standard encompasses service workers, web app manifests, and other APIs enabling installable, offline-capable web applications. Major browser vendors and standards bodies have collaborated to define and implement PWA capabilities.
PWA.summary_abuse: PWA as a standard has no notable abuse signal. It is a set of web platform technologies; however, PWA's installability feature has occasionally been misused by scammers to create fake apps that mimic legitimate banking or shopping apps to deceive users, though this is a misuse pattern rather than an inherent feature of the standard.

PWA Studio.history: PWA Studio is an open-source toolkit released by Magento (now Adobe Commerce) around 2018 to help developers build Progressive Web App storefronts on top of Magento 2 and Adobe Commerce. It is maintained by Adobe as part of the broader Adobe Commerce ecosystem.
PWA Studio.summary_abuse: PWA Studio has no notable abuse signal. It is an open-source ecommerce frontend toolkit used by Adobe Commerce developers, with no consumer-facing surface that would attract fraud operators.

Pxxl App.history: Pxxl App is a no-code platform that allows users to build and host web projects using custom subdomains on pxxl-managed domains. It is a small, recent product with very limited public history.
Pxxl App.summary_abuse: Pxxl App is a no-code website builder offering free hosting under pxxl-managed subdomains. Free hosting platforms with custom subdomain support are commonly exploited for phishing pages, fake storefronts, and spam landing pages. The low barrier to entry and anonymized hosting model elevates abuse potential, though no specific documented incidents exist for this platform specifically.

PyData Sphinx Theme.history: PyData Sphinx Theme is a Sphinx documentation theme for scientific Python, first released around 2020 as an open-source project.
PyData Sphinx Theme.summary_abuse: PyData Sphinx Theme has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer-focused open-source library with no consumer-facing infrastructure.

Pygments.history: Pygments is a Python syntax highlighting library created by Georg Brandl around 2006. It has become the de facto standard for syntax highlighting in Python documentation tools including Sphinx, Markdown renderers, and code editors. It supports over 500 programming languages.
Pygments.summary_abuse: Pygments has no notable abuse signal. It is a server-side syntax highlighting library with no network surface or consumer-facing infrastructure.

PyroCMS.history: PyroCMS is an open-source CMS built on the Laravel PHP framework, originally created around 2007. The project has gone through multiple major rewrites and is maintained as an independent open-source project.
PyroCMS.summary_abuse: PyroCMS has modest abuse potential as a self-hosted CMS that can host arbitrary content. No specific documented fraud incidents are known.

PyScript.history: PyScript was released by Anaconda in 2022 as a framework for running Python code in the browser via WebAssembly (Pyodide/WASM). It enables data scientists and Python developers to build interactive web applications without requiring a server-side Python environment.
PyScript.summary_abuse: PyScript has no notable abuse signal. It is a browser-based Python execution library with no hosting infrastructure or consumer-facing features that fraudsters would target.

Python.history: Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. Rossum guided development until stepping down as Benevolent Dictator For Life (BDFL) in 2018. Python is now governed by the Python Software Foundation (PSF). Python 2 was officially end-of-lifed in January 2020, pushing the ecosystem to Python 3.
Python.summary_abuse: Python has no notable abuse signal as a language. It is a general-purpose programming language used for an enormous range of legitimate applications. Malicious actors do write malware in Python, but this is true of all general-purpose languages and is not an abuse characteristic of Python itself.

PythonAnywhere.history: PythonAnywhere was founded in 2012 by Giles Thomas and Robert Smithson in London as an online Python IDE and hosting platform. It was later acquired by Anaconda Inc. It is widely used in education and for small Python web apps.
PythonAnywhere.summary_abuse: PythonAnywhere offers free Python hosting with a beginner-friendly free tier. Like other free hosting platforms, it can be used to host phishing pages or malicious scripts. The platform has an abuse reporting process and monitors for TOS violations. Abuse is a minor but documented fraction of its use.

PyWebIO.history: PyWebIO is a Framework for building browser-based Python UI, first released around 2019 as an open-source project.
PyWebIO.summary_abuse: PyWebIO has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer-focused open-source library with no consumer-facing infrastructure.

Pyze.history: Pyze is a digital analytics and engagement platform focused on measuring digital transformation initiatives. No founding year or ownership history is publicly documented.
Pyze.summary_abuse: Pyze has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B analytics platform with no consumer-facing features associated with fraud.
